She is not authorized to exceed 7.0× without returning to the Meridian board. She noted that 7.0× is near the median of the Frazier Whitmore comparable-transaction range (6.2×--8.1×, median 7.1×) and is therefore defensible to the Meridian board.
>
> • **Capital Calls (Section 4.03):** Cordero is willing to accept a total cure period of approximately 75 days (combining notice and cure), up from Meridian\'s opening position of 30 days and significantly below Kastor\'s request for 90 days. She is open to a structured penalty-interest mechanism during the cure period.
>
> • **Non-Compete (Section 11.01):** Cordero is prepared to move from Meridian\'s opening position of a 2-year post-exit non-compete to 18 months. She views non-compete duration as available concession currency and has authorized its use to secure gains on IP license-back and management control.
>
> • **IP License-Back (Section 12.05):** Cordero will not accept any structure that allows Kastor to sublicense Meridian-contributed IP to Meridian\'s North American competitors. This is an absolute red line with no authorized flexibility. Cordero described this as follows: \"If they can hand our formulations to Polaris or Trident, there is no deal.\" Limited flexibility exists on co-developed IP licensing outside North America and on the treatment of improvements to contributed IP, as detailed in Section VII below.

**[II. CONCESSION-PRIORITY MATRIX]{.underline}**

**[A. Priority Ranking]{.underline}**

The following priority ranking reflects both Cordero\'s negotiation mandate and our independent assessment of each provision\'s strategic and commercial significance to Meridian. The ranking is organized into three tiers, with Tier 1 representing the highest priority (minimal concession room) and Tier 3 representing the greatest flexibility (available as concession currency to secure gains on higher-priority items).

**Tier 1 --- Protect at All Costs (Highest Priority --- Minimal Concession Room)**

**IP License-Back (Section 12.05):** Meridian\'s 23 contributed IP items, independently valued at \$28.6M by Frazier Whitmore & Co., constitute the technological core of MKPoly\'s product portfolio. These assets include Meridian\'s proprietary CR-7 and CR-9 coating formulation systems, related process parameters, and associated trade secrets. Post-exit sublicensing restrictions are Cordero\'s stated red line and Meridian\'s single highest-priority contested term. No sublicensing of Meridian-contributed IP to Meridian\'s North American competitors under any circumstances. Limited concession room exists on co-developed IP licensing and improvements allocation (see Section VII), but these concessions must be carefully structured to prevent indirect access to Meridian\'s contributed formulations.

**Management Control (Section 5.01):** Meridian\'s 55% equity interest must be reflected in the JV\'s governance structure. Board composition and the CEO\'s casting vote in deadlock situations are core mechanisms for maintaining operational direction of MKPoly. Meridian\'s contributed IP is embedded in every JV product line, and technical management by Meridian-aligned leadership is essential to preserving IP integrity, product quality, and customer relationships. Concession room is limited to accepting Kastor protective veto rights on narrow Reserved Matters or, at the outer boundary, a 3-3 board with structural protections (see Section III).

**Tier 2 --- Defend but Trade Selectively (Moderate Priority)**

**Capital Calls & Dilution (Section 4.03):** Meridian\'s strong preference is for uncapped immediate pro-rata dilution upon failure to fund capital calls, which incentivizes prompt funding and rewards Meridian\'s superior financial capacity. However, Meridian has meaningful room to negotiate cure periods, penalty-interest mechanisms, and limited loan alternatives, provided that the economic terms preserve the incentive to fund. This provision is a moderate priority: important to Meridian\'s long-term economic position in the JV, but not existential. Cordero has authorized movement to approximately 75 days total cure, and the provision can serve as a middle-ground item where both parties can claim concessions.

**Tier 3 --- Greater Flexibility (Available as Concession Currency)**

**Non-Compete Duration (Section 11.01):** Meridian\'s opening position is a 2-year post-exit non-compete covering all specialty polymer coatings in North America. Cordero is prepared to move to 18 months --- a 25% reduction in duration that represents a significant, visible concession. Non-compete duration is the primary concession lever available to Meridian to secure gains on Tier 1 items, particularly IP license-back. The concession is low-cost to Meridian because 18 months falls within the safe harbor for enforceability under both Delaware and Texas law and provides adequate competitive protection.

**Exit Valuation Multiple (Sections 10.03--10.04):** Meridian\'s opening position is a 6.5× trailing 12-month EBITDA multiple, which is at the conservative end of the Frazier Whitmore comparable-transaction range (6.2×--8.1×, median 7.1×). Cordero is prepared to move to 7.0×. Movement from 6.5× to 7.0× is a 7.7% increase in the exit valuation multiple, which can be presented to Kastor as a meaningful concession. The actual cost to Meridian depends on MKPoly\'s EBITDA at exit and whether Meridian is the buyer or seller --- if Meridian is the buyer, a higher multiple is costly, but if Meridian is the seller, the higher multiple benefits Meridian. On balance, the exit valuation multiple is a relatively low-cost concession given the uncertainty of its ultimate direction.

**[B. Concession Mapping Guide]{.underline}**

The following table illustrates how concessions on Tier 3 items can be strategically deployed to fund asks on Tier 1 items. Each row identifies a specific concession Meridian can offer, the corresponding ask it supports, and the net value assessment for Meridian.

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Concession Offered (Tier 3)**                                                                  **Ask It Funds (Tier 1/2)**                                                                                                                                                             **Net Value Assessment**
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Non-compete: 2 years → 18 months (6-month reduction)                                             IP License-Back: Exclusive Meridian contributed IP reversion with no Kastor sublicensing; co-developed IP sublicensing restricted to non-Restricted Competitors outside North America   **Strongly positive.** 6-month non-compete reduction costs Meridian minimal competitive protection; securing sublicensing restrictions protects \$28.6M IP contribution and Meridian\'s North American market position.

  Exit multiple: 6.5× → 6.8× EBITDA (initial move)                                                 Management Control: 3-2 board composition with narrow Reserved Matters supermajority                                                                                                    **Moderately positive.** 0.3× multiple increase is within comparable range and costs Meridian only if it is the buyer at exit. Securing 3-2 board ensures operational control consistent with 55% equity.

  Exit multiple: 6.8× → 7.0× EBITDA (second move)                                                  Management Control: If 3-2 rejected, secure Meridian chair casting vote on all non-Reserved Matters in 3-3 structure                                                                    **Moderately positive.** Total 0.5× move from opening brings Meridian near comparable median (7.1×). Casting vote in 3-3 structure preserves effective operational tiebreak.

  Non-compete scope: all specialty polymer coatings → JV product lines + JV-developed technology   IP License-Back: Restricted Competitors schedule broadened to top 15--20 North American competitors and affiliates                                                                      **Neutral to positive.** Scope narrowing reduces Kastor\'s burden without materially weakening Meridian\'s protection; broader Restricted Competitors list provides equivalent protection through sublicensing restrictions.

  Capital call cure: 30 days → 60 days with penalty interest                                       IP License-Back: No concession on contributed IP sublicensing restrictions                                                                                                              **Positive.** Cure-period extension is within Cordero\'s authorized range and costs Meridian the time value of delayed funding (mitigated by penalty interest). Holding firm on contributed IP sublicensing protects core competitive position.

  Exit trigger events: add deadlock and involuntary transfer                                       Management Control: Deadlock resolution tiered structure with CEO casting vote as final tier                                                                                            **Positive.** Conceding trigger events costs Meridian little (deadlock trigger is consistent with tiered deadlock resolution; involuntary transfer trigger protects both parties). Tiered deadlock resolution preserves Meridian\'s ultimate tiebreak.
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**[C. Red Lines Summary]{.underline}**

The following positions are absolute walk-away triggers. If Kastor\'s final position crosses any of these lines, Meridian should terminate negotiations and pursue its BATNA (see Section X).

1\. **No sublicensing by Kastor of Meridian-contributed IP to Schedule 12.05 Restricted Competitors.** This prohibition applies regardless of territory, regardless of whether the sublicense is direct or indirect, and regardless of whether the Restricted Competitor is the end user or an intermediary. Any structure --- including purportedly \"territorial\" sublicensing --- that creates a pathway for Meridian\'s proprietary coating formulations to reach Polaris Coatings Corp., Endurance Surface Technologies, Trident Polymer Systems, or any other entity on the Restricted Competitors schedule is unacceptable.

2\. **Meridian must retain effective management control.** This means either (a) a board majority (3-2 composition), or (b) if 3-3 composition, a Meridian-appointed chairperson with casting vote on all non-Reserved Matters and sole Meridian authority to appoint the CEO and CFO. A governance structure in which Kastor holds an effective veto over ordinary-course business decisions is inconsistent with Meridian\'s 55% equity stake and is a deal-breaker.

3\. **Dilution cap cannot exceed the range Meridian can accept.** A dilution cap below 20% per annum effectively subsidizes a non-funding partner and eliminates Meridian\'s economic incentive to pursue additional capital investment. Combined with a governance structure that gives Kastor veto power, a low dilution cap would allow Kastor to block growth initiatives and resist funding without consequence.

4\. **No cumulative concession structure that leaves Meridian with majority equity but minority effective control.** Even if no single red line is crossed, a combination of concessions across all five provisions that, taken together, gives Kastor effective veto power, caps Meridian\'s dilution upside, broadly licenses Meridian\'s IP, and constrains Meridian\'s post-exit competitive flexibility would be unacceptable. Cordero and the deal team should evaluate the aggregate effect of any package proposal before accepting.

**[III. CONTESTED PROVISION #1 --- MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE & GOVERNANCE (Section 5.01)]{.underline}**

**[A. Meridian\'s Opening / Preferred Position]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s preferred governance structure, as set forth in its initial markup of Section 5.01, is as follows:

> • **Board Composition:** The Management Committee (the \"Board\") shall consist of five (5) members --- three (3) appointed by Meridian (the \"Meridian Designees\") and two (2) appointed by Kastor (the \"Kastor Designees\"). Each member shall appoint and remove its designees at its sole discretion.
>
> • **Voting:** All Board actions shall require approval by simple majority vote of the members present at a duly convened meeting at which a quorum is present. A quorum shall consist of four (4) members, including at least one Meridian Designee and one Kastor Designee.
>
> • **CEO Casting Vote:** In the event of a deadlock (defined as a matter on which the Board is unable to reach a majority vote after two successive meetings at which the matter is considered), the Chief Executive Officer, who shall be appointed by Meridian, shall hold a casting vote to resolve the deadlock.
>
> • **Officer Appointments:** The CEO and Chief Financial Officer shall be appointed by Meridian. The Chief Operating Officer and Chief Technology Officer shall be appointed jointly by the Board (simple majority vote). All other officers below the C-suite level shall be appointed by the CEO in the ordinary course of business.

**Rationale:** Meridian\'s 55% equity interest and the fact that its contributed IP (\$28.6M, representing the JV\'s core technological platform) forms the foundation of MKPoly\'s product lines justify proportionate governance control. In the specialty chemicals industry, joint ventures in which one partner holds a clear majority equity position --- particularly when that partner has contributed the venture\'s core technology --- consistently grant governance control to the majority holder. The 3-2 board composition is the standard mechanism for achieving this, and Meridian\'s proposal is fully consistent with industry norms and Delaware LLC law, which permits broad flexibility in allocating governance rights through the LLC agreement.

Moreover, the CEO casting vote serves a practical function: it provides a clear, efficient deadlock resolution mechanism that avoids the delay and expense of mediation, arbitration, or forced buyout proceedings. The alternative --- protracted deadlock resolution processes --- creates operational uncertainty that is harmful to MKPoly and its employees, customers, and suppliers.

**[B. Fallback Ladder]{.underline}**

**First Concession --- Protective Veto Rights (Narrow Reserved Matters)**

If Kastor insists on enhanced protective rights --- either by demanding a sixth board seat, by demanding supermajority requirements on all Board actions, or by conditioning acceptance of the 3-2 structure on broader minority protections --- Meridian\'s first fallback is to maintain the 3-2 board composition but concede supermajority voting (requiring the affirmative vote of at least four members, which necessarily includes at least one Kastor Designee) on a narrowly defined list of Reserved Matters.

The Reserved Matters list should be limited to the following:

> \(a\) Any amendment, modification, or restatement of the LLC Agreement or the Certificate of Formation;
>
> \(b\) Voluntary dissolution, winding up, or filing of a petition under any federal or state bankruptcy, insolvency, or receivership statute;
>
> \(c\) Sale, transfer, or other disposition of all or substantially all of MKPoly\'s assets in a single transaction or a series of related transactions;
>
> \(d\) Any single-transaction capital expenditure (or series of related capital expenditures within any 12-month period) exceeding \$5,000,000;
>
> \(e\) Admission of any new member to MKPoly, or any issuance of new membership interests to any person or entity other than the existing members on a pro-rata basis;
>
> \(f\) Entry into, or material amendment of, any transaction between MKPoly and any member or affiliate of a member (related-party transactions); and
>
> \(g\) Any material change to the nature or scope of MKPoly\'s business as described in Section 2.04 of the LLC Agreement.

**Cordero\'s internal guidance supports this concession** provided the Reserved Matters list does not expand to include any of the following: ordinary-course operating decisions, approval of annual operating budgets, hiring or termination of personnel below the C-suite level, entry into customer or supplier contracts in the ordinary course of business, pricing decisions, or product development priorities. These are operational matters that must remain within the CEO\'s delegated authority or subject to simple-majority Board vote.

**Presentation strategy:** This concession should be presented as a meaningful move by Meridian. Cordero should emphasize that the Reserved Matters list gives Kastor an effective veto on every existential decision --- dissolution, asset sales, new partners, major capital commitments, and related-party transactions. These are precisely the decisions where a minority partner\'s protective interests are legitimate. In contrast, routine operational decisions must be made efficiently by management and should not require minority consent.

**Second Concession --- Equal Board with Structural Protections (3-3 with Casting Vote)**

If Kastor refuses the 3-2 structure even with Reserved Matters protections --- and if the negotiation dynamic requires further movement on governance to secure Kastor\'s acceptance of Meridian\'s Tier 1 position on IP license-back --- Meridian\'s second fallback is a 3-3 board composition with the following structural protections:

> \(a\) **Chairperson with Casting Vote:** The Chairperson of the Board shall be appointed by Meridian and shall hold a casting vote on all matters that are not Reserved Matters. The Chairperson\'s casting vote shall be exercisable only after the Board has been unable to reach a majority vote on the matter at two successive duly convened meetings (i.e., the same deadlock threshold as the CEO casting vote in Meridian\'s opening position).
>
> \(b\) **Officer Appointment Authority:** Meridian retains the sole right to appoint the Chief Executive Officer and the Chief Financial Officer. Joint Board approval (simple majority, with the Chairperson\'s casting vote available) is required for the Chief Operating Officer and Chief Technology Officer. All other officers and senior management below the C-suite are appointed by the CEO.
>
> \(c\) **Reserved Matters:** Reserved Matters are defined narrowly per the list in the First Concession above. Supermajority voting (requiring at least four affirmative votes) applies only to Reserved Matters.
>
> \(d\) **Delegated Management Authority:** Day-to-day management authority is delegated to the CEO without Board approval for any action or expenditure that (i) is within the approved annual operating budget and (ii) does not involve a single-transaction expenditure exceeding \$1,000,000. This delegation ensures that operational decisions are not subject to Board-level deadlock.

**Rationale:** Even at 3-3 board composition, operational control remains effectively with Meridian through two mechanisms: (i) the casting vote on non-Reserved Matters, which gives Meridian the tiebreak on any operating dispute, and (ii) sole authority to appoint the CEO and CFO, which ensures that the officers with day-to-day management authority are Meridian-aligned. The 3-3 structure is a cosmetic concession to Kastor\'s demand for \"equal partnership\" while preserving the substance of Meridian\'s control.

**Presentation strategy:** If Meridian needs to move to this position, Cordero should present it as a package with IP license-back. The message should be: \"We are willing to give you equal representation on the Board because we trust this partnership. But equal representation must come with recognition that our IP --- which is the reason this JV exists --- needs to be protected by strong sublicensing restrictions.\" This frames the governance concession as an act of partnership confidence rather than weakness.

**Walk-Away Floor.** Meridian cannot accept a governance structure that combines all of the following elements:

> \(a\) 3-3 board composition;
>
> \(b\) Supermajority voting requirements on matters beyond the narrow Reserved Matters list (e.g., annual budget approval, material contracts, product development priorities);
>
> \(c\) No Meridian casting vote on non-Reserved Matters; and
>
> \(d\) No sole Meridian authority to appoint the CEO.

A structure with these features would give Kastor an effective veto over routine management decisions despite holding only 45% of the equity. This is inconsistent with the economic terms of the deal and negates the premium Meridian has placed on control. In such a structure, Meridian would have majority equity ownership but minority effective control --- a result that is commercially irrational and inconsistent with Cordero\'s mandate.

**[C. Commercial Rationale]{.underline}**

Management control is critical to Meridian for three independent reasons, each of which should be articulated to Kastor as appropriate during the August 11 meeting:

**1. IP Integrity and Technical Direction.** Meridian\'s proprietary coating formulations --- particularly the CR-7 and CR-9 systems --- are embedded in every JV product line. These formulations require precise technical management to maintain performance specifications, quality standards, and regulatory compliance. Meridian has developed deep institutional expertise in the application and optimization of these formulations over more than 15 years of commercial deployment. Kastor, while an experienced chemicals company, does not have this expertise. Management control ensures that the CEO and technical leadership have the authority to make product-development and quality-control decisions without the delay or compromise that would result from Board-level deadlock with a partner that lacks the relevant technical knowledge.

**2. Customer Relationship Continuity.** Meridian\'s customer relationships in the North American specialty coatings market are relationship-driven and built on long-standing trust between Meridian\'s commercial team and key procurement decision-makers at major aerospace and industrial customers. These relationships are a significant component of MKPoly\'s expected revenue base. Continuity of commercial strategy --- including pricing, product positioning, and key-account management --- requires that Meridian\'s leadership team have operational authority. A governance structure that subjects these decisions to minority-partner approval or Board-level deadlock creates uncertainty that could erode customer confidence.

**3. Industry Norms.** Comparable JV structures in the specialty chemicals industry consistently grant governance control to the majority equity holder. As discussed in Section VIII below, all three comparable transactions involve board compositions that either directly reflect equity ownership (3-2 in Comparable #1) or provide the majority partner with a structural tiebreak (chair casting vote in Comparable #2). Kastor\'s demand for equal governance despite a 45% equity position is outside the range of market norms.

**[D. Anticipated Kastor Arguments & Recommended Responses]{.underline}**

**Kastor Argument 1 --- Contribution Parity**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor will argue that its total contribution (\$43.8M, comprising \$41.8M cash and \$2.0M supply-agreement value) exceeds Meridian\'s total contribution (\$42.9M, comprising \$28.6M IP and \$14.3M cash). Kastor will contend that a near-equal --- and indeed slightly higher --- financial stake warrants equal governance. Kastor\'s counsel, Rennert Krieger Solano, has made this argument in its July 18 redline comments on Section 5.01, characterizing the proposed 3-2 structure as \"inconsistent with the parties\' substantially equal economic commitments.\"

**Recommended response:** Acknowledge the financial proximity of the two contributions but draw a sharp distinction between the nature of the contributions. Meridian\'s IP contribution creates the JV\'s core competitive advantage. Without Meridian\'s coating formulations, MKPoly has no differentiated product offering. Kastor\'s contribution, while substantial, is primarily cash --- a fungible asset that could, in principle, be raised from other sources. Meridian\'s IP, by contrast, is irreplaceable: it represents over 15 years of R&D investment and cannot be replicated or purchased on the open market. Governance control is the mechanism by which Meridian protects the value of this irreplaceable contribution. This is why the parties agreed to a 55/45 equity split despite near-equal dollar contributions --- the equity premium reflects the strategic value of Meridian\'s IP, and governance should track equity.

**Kastor Argument 2 --- Deadlock Risk Without Equal Representation**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor will argue that a 3-2 board with a CEO casting vote gives Meridian unilateral control over all Board decisions and leaves Kastor without a meaningful voice in the venture\'s direction. Kastor will characterize this structure as a \"controlled subsidiary,\" not a true joint venture, and will argue that Kastor\'s board should not agree to invest €38M in a venture over which it has no governance influence.

**Recommended response:** Offer the first-concession position --- supermajority voting on a narrow list of Reserved Matters --- to demonstrate that Kastor retains meaningful protective veto power on existential decisions. The distinction between protective rights and affirmative management control should be clearly articulated: protective rights (veto on dissolution, asset sales, new partners, major capital expenditures) are appropriate for a minority partner and give Kastor the ability to block decisions that would fundamentally change the venture\'s structure or risk profile. Affirmative management control (the ability to direct day-to-day operations, set commercial strategy, make hiring decisions, and resolve operating disputes) is appropriate for the majority equity partner. This distinction is well-established in JV practice and is the standard framework in Delaware LLC agreements for majority-minority ventures.

Cordero should avoid characterizing the 3-2 structure as giving Meridian \"unilateral\" control. Instead, frame it as \"operational leadership with partner protection\" --- Meridian leads the venture\'s operations, but Kastor has a voice on the Board and a veto on the decisions that matter most to a minority partner.

**Kastor Argument 3 --- Deadlock Resolution**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor\'s position on deadlock resolution, as reflected in JVA v.4, is a two-step process: (i) non-binding mediation, followed by (ii) forced buyout at fair market value if mediation fails. Kastor views this structure as preferable to a CEO casting vote because it preserves both parties\' ability to exit a dysfunctional partnership rather than having one party\'s appointee unilaterally resolve disputes. Kastor\'s counsel has argued that the CEO casting vote creates \"moral hazard\" by allowing Meridian to manufacture deadlocks and then resolve them in its favor.

**Recommended response:** Meridian can accept an escalation-based deadlock resolution mechanism as a fallback, but the structure must preserve Meridian\'s ultimate tiebreak on operational matters. The recommended tiered structure is as follows:

> **Step 1 --- Principals\' Negotiation (15 Business Days).** Upon declaration of a deadlock by either party, the CEOs (or designated senior executives) of Meridian and Kastor shall engage in good-faith negotiation for a period of fifteen (15) business days to attempt resolution. During this period, the status quo shall be maintained, and neither party shall take unilateral action on the deadlocked matter.
>
> **Step 2 --- Mediation (30 Calendar Days).** If the deadlock is not resolved during the principals\' negotiation period, either party may initiate non-binding mediation administered by a mutually agreed neutral mediator. Meridian recommends designating the American Arbitration Association (\"AAA\") Commercial Mediation panel in Houston, Texas as the default mediation administrator. The mediation period shall not exceed thirty (30) calendar days from the appointment of the mediator.
>
> **Step 3 --- Resolution (Following Mediation).** If the deadlock persists after the mediation period:
>
> • For *operational matters* (i.e., matters that are not Reserved Matters), the CEO\'s casting vote shall resolve the deadlock. This ensures that MKPoly is never operationally paralyzed by an unresolved disagreement.
>
> • For *Reserved Matters*, if the deadlock persists after the full escalation process, either party may initiate the buy-sell mechanism under Section 11 of the LLC Agreement.

This structure gives Kastor the escalation process it wants --- principals\' negotiation and mediation before any unilateral resolution --- while preserving Meridian\'s ultimate tiebreak on operational issues. The buy-sell mechanism as a final resort for Reserved Matters deadlocks is a reasonable concession because Reserved Matters involve existential decisions where a forced exit is preferable to indefinite paralysis.

**Critical caution:** Meridian should resist forced buyout as a *first-tier* deadlock remedy (i.e., applicable to all deadlocks, not just Reserved Matters). A structure in which any deadlock --- including routine operating disagreements --- triggers a buyout right creates perverse incentives for Kastor to manufacture deadlocks to trigger exit at favorable valuation, particularly if MKPoly is performing well and the exit valuation methodology produces an attractive price. The tiered structure above confines the buyout trigger to Reserved Matters deadlocks that survive two prior escalation steps, significantly reducing this risk.

**[IV. CONTESTED PROVISION #2 --- CAPITAL CALLS & DILUTION (Section 4.03)]{.underline}**

**[A. Meridian\'s Opening / Preferred Position]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s preferred capital-call and dilution structure, as set forth in its initial markup of Section 4.03, is as follows:

> • **Capital Call Authority:** The Board may authorize capital calls upon the members at any time by majority vote. Capital calls shall be pro-rata in proportion to the members\' respective equity interests unless otherwise agreed unanimously.
>
> • **Funding Obligation:** Each member shall fund its pro-rata share of any duly authorized capital call within thirty (30) calendar days of receipt of written notice from MKPoly\'s CFO specifying the amount, purpose, and due date of the capital call.
>
> • **Dilution on Failure to Fund:** If a member fails to fund its pro-rata share of a capital call within the 30-day funding period, the non-funding member\'s equity interest shall be immediately and automatically diluted on a pro-rata basis. Dilution shall be calculated based on the capital call amount relative to total post-call capitalization at fair market value, as determined by MKPoly\'s auditor (Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP).
>
> • **No Dilution Cap:** Dilution shall be uncapped --- there shall be no limit on the aggregate dilution that a non-funding member may experience.
>
> • **No Loan Alternative:** There shall be no alternative mechanism by which a non-funding member may borrow from the funding member in lieu of accepting dilution.

**Rationale:** Immediate, uncapped dilution upon failure to fund is the strongest possible incentive for both parties to meet their capital obligations promptly. Meridian has the financial capacity and willingness to fund capital calls and benefits strategically from any dilution of a non-funding partner --- both through increased equity ownership and through enhanced governance control (as equity-based governance would shift in Meridian\'s favor). A dilution cap or loan alternative weakens this incentive and creates the risk that Kastor will defer or decline funding while retaining governance rights disproportionate to its economic stake.

**[B. Fallback Ladder]{.underline}**

**First Concession --- 45-Day Cure Period**

If Kastor presses for additional time to fund capital calls --- and Kastor will, citing FX conversion and Swiss regulatory requirements (see Section IV.D below) --- Meridian\'s first concession is to extend the cure period from 30 days to 45 calendar days. Combined with the 15-day notice period before the cure period begins, this provides a total of 60 days from the initial capital call notice to the dilution trigger.

**Rationale:** A 45-day cure period allows Kastor reasonable time to arrange USD funding, including FX conversion of €-denominated funds and any required Swiss regulatory filings (FINMA notifications, if applicable). It is significantly more generous than Meridian\'s opening position and should be presented to Kastor as a meaningful move. At the same time, 45 days is materially shorter than Kastor\'s requested 90-day cure period and preserves the urgency of the funding obligation.

**Presentation strategy:** Cordero should present the 45-day cure period as a concession made in recognition of Kastor\'s operational realities as a Swiss-headquartered multinational. Emphasize that Meridian is accommodating Kastor\'s specific circumstances --- not weakening the dilution mechanism --- and that the cure period applies equally to both parties.

**Second Concession --- 60-Day Cure with Penalty Interest**

If Kastor rejects 45 days and continues to insist on a 90-day cure period, Meridian\'s second concession is to move to a 60-day cure period with automatic penalty interest accruing during the cure period. The terms would be as follows:

> \(a\) The non-funding member shall have sixty (60) calendar days from the expiration of the 15-day notice period (i.e., a total of 75 days from the initial capital call notice) to fund its pro-rata share of the capital call.
>
> \(b\) During the 60-day cure period, penalty interest shall accrue automatically on the unfunded amount at a rate equal to the greater of (i) the Secured Overnight Financing Rate (\"SOFR\") plus 500 basis points per annum, or (ii) 10% per annum.
>
> \(c\) Penalty interest shall be payable to MKPoly (for the benefit of the funding member) upon funding or upon dilution, as applicable.
>
> \(d\) If the non-funding member funds its full pro-rata share plus accrued penalty interest within the 60-day cure period, no dilution shall occur.
>
> \(e\) If the non-funding member fails to fund within the 60-day cure period, automatic pro-rata dilution shall occur as specified in Meridian\'s opening position, and all accrued penalty interest shall remain payable.

**Rationale:** The 75-day total period (15-day notice + 60-day cure) falls squarely within Cordero\'s authorized range of \"approximately 75 days.\" The penalty-interest mechanism serves a dual function: it compensates MKPoly (and, indirectly, the funding member) for the time value of delayed capital, and it imposes sufficient economic cost on late funding to discourage strategic delay. At SOFR + 500 bps or 10% (whichever is greater), the penalty rate is meaningfully above market and creates a strong incentive to fund promptly.

**Third Concession --- Loan Mechanism with Punitive Terms**

If Kastor insists on a loan alternative --- Kastor\'s position in JVA v.4 is that a non-funding member should have the option to borrow from the funding member at a \"market rate\" (defined as SOFR + 200 bps) rather than accept dilution --- Meridian can accept a loan mechanism, but only with terms sufficiently punitive to ensure that dilution remains the economically rational choice for any party acting in good faith. The loan mechanism would be structured as follows:

> \(a\) **Interest Rate:** The loan shall bear interest at a rate equal to SOFR plus 800 basis points per annum, compounding quarterly. This rate is not a market rate --- it is deliberately punitive and reflects the involuntary nature of the lending (the funding member did not choose to lend; it chose to invest equity). The premium over market rates compensates the funding member for credit risk, liquidity impairment, and the opportunity cost of capital deployed as debt rather than equity.
>
> \(b\) **Mandatory Repayment:** The loan shall be repayable in full (principal plus accrued interest) within twelve (12) months of the date of disbursement. No extensions, no refinancing, no amortization schedule --- full repayment is required in a single lump sum.
>
> \(c\) **Security Interest:** The borrowing member shall grant a first-priority security interest in its entire JV membership interest to the lending member as collateral for the loan. UCC financing statements shall be filed promptly upon disbursement.
>
> \(d\) **Automatic Conversion to Equity on Default:** If the borrowing member fails to repay the loan in full within the 12-month repayment period, the outstanding loan balance (principal plus accrued interest) shall automatically convert to equity, resulting in dilution of the borrowing member\'s interest. The conversion shall be calculated on the same basis as dilution for failure to fund a capital call.
>
> \(e\) **Single-Use Limitation:** The loan alternative shall be available to any member only once in any 24-month period. If a member fails to fund a capital call and elects the loan alternative, any subsequent failure to fund within the following 24 months shall result in automatic dilution without the option of a loan. This prevents serial use of the loan mechanism to defer indefinitely.
>
> \(f\) **No Set-Off:** The borrowing member shall not be entitled to set off any amounts owed to it by MKPoly or the lending member against the loan repayment obligation.

**Rationale:** This structure technically gives Kastor its requested loan alternative --- Kastor can point to the loan mechanism and tell its board that it has an alternative to dilution. But the economic terms are designed to make the loan option unattractive relative to prompt funding. At SOFR + 800 bps with mandatory 12-month repayment, the annualized cost of borrowing will likely exceed 12--14%, which is well above the expected return on MKPoly equity. A rational party would choose to fund rather than borrow at these rates. And if the borrowing party defaults on the loan, the automatic equity conversion ensures that the end result is the same as dilution --- with the added benefit that the lending member has earned punitive interest during the loan period.

**Walk-Away Floor on Dilution Cap**

Kastor\'s proposed 10% per annum dilution cap (Section 4.03(d) of JVA v.4) is unacceptable and should be firmly rejected. A 10% annual cap on dilution means that even if Kastor fails to fund multiple successive capital calls, its equity interest can decrease by no more than 10 percentage points in any given year. For a partner starting at 45%, this means Kastor could decline to fund capital calls for nearly five years before being diluted to zero --- all while retaining governance rights (including Reserved Matters veto) disproportionate to its remaining economic interest.

The practical effect of a 10% cap is to allow Kastor to free-ride on Meridian-funded growth. Meridian funds the capital calls, MKPoly\'s enterprise value increases, and Kastor\'s retained equity interest benefits from that increase even though Kastor did not contribute to it. This is precisely the dynamic that uncapped dilution is designed to prevent.

**Meridian\'s walk-away position is a dilution cap no lower than 20% per annum.** A 20% cap still constrains Meridian\'s ability to dilute Kastor aggressively in any single year, but it ensures that a partner that consistently fails to fund will be diluted to a minority position within two to three years --- at which point governance rights would shift accordingly. The 20% cap should be presented to Kastor as a significant concession from Meridian\'s uncapped opening position, and Cordero should resist any further movement below 20%.

For reference, Comparable Transaction #2 (Section VIII.B) involved a 15% annual dilution cap, but that transaction also included an automatic forced-sale mechanism if dilution exceeded 15% in any 24-month period --- effectively converting the cap into a put/call right. Meridian would consider a similar forced-sale mechanism at the 20% threshold, but this introduces additional complexity that should be reserved for the working-team level.

**[C. Commercial Rationale]{.underline}**

The capital call and dilution provisions are critically important to Meridian\'s long-term economic position in MKPoly, even though they are classified as Tier 2 (moderate priority) for negotiation purposes. Several commercial factors drive this importance:

**1. Anticipated Follow-On Capital Requirements.** MKPoly\'s Q1 2026 operational launch is predicated on an initial capitalization of \$86.7M, but internal projections (prepared by Meridian\'s finance team, not shared with Kastor) indicate that follow-on capital calls of \$15M--\$25M are likely within the first 24 months of operations to fund capacity expansion, additional R&D, and working capital for customer ramp-up. Meridian has allocated reserves for these calls; whether Kastor has done so is unknown.

**2. Kastor\'s Capital Allocation Constraints.** Kastor is a global chemicals conglomerate with multiple JVs, subsidiaries, and capital projects. Its cash contribution of €38M to MKPoly, while substantial in absolute terms, represents a material allocation of Kastor\'s annual capital budget. Our due diligence indicates that Kastor is concurrently funding a €120M expansion of its Ludwigshafen, Germany manufacturing complex and a €45M JV in South Korea. Whether Kastor can or will fund additional capital calls to MKPoly in competition with these other demands is uncertain.

**3. Meridian\'s Financial Capacity as a Strategic Advantage.** Meridian\'s ability and willingness to fund capital calls is one of its strongest strategic assets in this JV. A robust dilution mechanism translates Meridian\'s financial commitment into equity upside: if Meridian funds and Kastor does not, Meridian\'s equity percentage increases, its governance control strengthens, and its share of exit proceeds grows. A weak dilution mechanism (capped at 10%, with a market-rate loan alternative) neutralizes this advantage by allowing Kastor to retain its position without funding.

**4. Alignment of Incentives.** At a fundamental level, the dilution mechanism is about aligning incentives: both partners should have a strong economic reason to fund the venture\'s growth. A well-designed dilution mechanism ensures that a partner that believes in MKPoly\'s future will fund it, and a partner that does not believe will accept the consequences of not funding. A dilution cap and market-rate loan tell a different story: they tell a non-funding partner that the consequences of inaction are minimal. Meridian should not accept that message.

**[D. Anticipated Kastor Arguments & Recommended Responses]{.underline}**

**Kastor Argument 1 --- FX Conversion Delays**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor will argue that converting large sums from EUR or CHF to USD requires multiple institutional approvals, FX hedging arrangements, and potentially Swiss regulatory notifications (FINMA). Kastor\'s treasury operates on €-denominated cycles, and a 30-day cure period is operationally impractical for a €10M+ capital call. Kastor will cite its 90-day request as reflecting genuine operational constraints, not strategic delay.

**Recommended response:** Acknowledge the FX complexity --- this is a legitimate operational issue, and Meridian should not dismiss it. However, note that the 15-day notice period plus a 60-day cure period provides a total of 75 days from initial notification to the dilution trigger. For a sophisticated multinational chemicals company with a treasury function that routinely manages multi-currency transactions in excess of €100M annually, 75 days is ample time to arrange USD funding.

Additionally, Cordero should offer a specific accommodation: MKPoly will accept cure-period funding denominated in EUR at a locked exchange rate (defined as the ECB reference rate on the date of the capital call notice), with conversion to USD handled by MKPoly\'s treasury at MKPoly\'s cost. This eliminates Kastor\'s FX conversion burden entirely and removes the operational justification for a 90-day cure period.

**Kastor Argument 2 --- Dilution as Punitive**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor will characterize uncapped dilution as a mechanism designed to allow Meridian to opportunistically seize majority control by issuing capital calls that Kastor cannot or does not wish to fund. Kastor\'s counsel has used the term \"creeping expropriation\" in prior working-team sessions to describe this concern. Kastor will argue that its €38M initial contribution should be protected against disproportionate dilution and that a reasonable cap ensures both parties retain meaningful equity positions.

**Recommended response:** Emphasize the symmetry of the dilution mechanism: it applies equally to both parties. If Meridian fails to fund a capital call, Meridian is diluted on the same terms. The real question is not whether dilution is punitive but whether both partners are genuinely committed to funding MKPoly\'s growth. A dilution cap signals willingness to accept free-riding --- it tells the funding partner that the non-funding partner will retain a substantial equity position regardless of its failure to contribute.

Present the 20% annual cap as a reasonable middle ground: it protects Kastor against extreme single-year dilution while ensuring that persistent non-funding has meaningful consequences. Reference Comparable Transaction #2 (Section VIII.B), where dilution was capped at 15% per annum but combined with an automatic forced-sale mechanism above that threshold --- effectively making the cap a bridge to exit rather than a shield for non-funding.

Regarding \"creeping expropriation\": capital calls require Board approval by majority vote. Under either the 3-2 or 3-3 board structure, a capital call requires at least one Kastor Designee\'s vote if it is a Reserved Matter (depending on the final Reserved Matters list) or, at minimum, is subject to Board-level scrutiny. Meridian cannot unilaterally issue capital calls to force dilution.

**Kastor Argument 3 --- Loan Alternative at Market Rate**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor will propose a loan mechanism at SOFR + 200 bps (or a similar market rate), arguing that this gives the non-funding member a commercially reasonable alternative to dilution while ensuring the funding member is compensated for its capital. Kastor will characterize this as standard in European JV practice and will argue that a market-rate loan is more equitable than dilution because it preserves the parties\' agreed equity split.

**Recommended response:** A market-rate loan eliminates the economic incentive to fund capital calls. If Kastor can borrow at SOFR + 200 bps (currently approximately 7.3% annualized) rather than invest equity that is expected to generate returns of 15--20% (per Meridian\'s internal projections), Kastor would rationally choose to borrow every time. The loan alternative, at market rates, is not an alternative to dilution --- it is an alternative to investing. It allows Kastor to maintain its equity position, retain its governance rights, and benefit from MKPoly\'s growth, all while paying a below-equity-return cost. This is the very definition of free-riding.

If Meridian concedes a loan mechanism, the terms must be sufficiently punitive to preserve the incentive to fund. Present the Third Concession (SOFR + 800 bps, 12-month mandatory repayment, security interest, automatic equity conversion on default, single-use limitation) and emphasize that automatic conversion to equity on default ensures the loan is a bridge --- a short-term accommodation for a partner with temporary liquidity constraints --- not an escape hatch from capital commitments.

**[V. CONTESTED PROVISION #3 --- NON-COMPETE COVENANTS (Section 11.01)]{.underline}**

**[A. Meridian\'s Opening / Preferred Position]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s preferred non-compete structure, as set forth in its initial markup of Section 11.01, is as follows:

> • **Duration:** Two (2) years following the effective date of a member\'s exit from MKPoly (whether by sale of its membership interest, dissolution, or termination of the JVA).
>
> • **Scope:** All specialty polymer coatings, including but not limited to protective coatings, performance coatings, industrial coatings, and aerospace coatings, regardless of specific product line or application.
>
> • **Geography:** North America (defined as the United States, Canada, and Mexico, including all territories and possessions).
>
> • **Covered Persons:** The exiting member and all of its affiliates, officers, directors, and employees who had access to MKPoly confidential information during the JV term.
>
> • **Permitted Activities:** The non-compete does not restrict the exiting member from (a) owning less than 5% of the publicly traded securities of any company engaged in specialty polymer coatings, (b) continuing to operate any business that the exiting member operated prior to the formation of MKPoly, provided that such business does not expand into new specialty polymer coating product lines after exit, or (c) engaging in the manufacture or sale of general-purpose (non-specialty) polymer products.

**Rationale:** Meridian\'s contributed IP and customer relationships in North American specialty coatings require meaningful protection against immediate competition by a former JV partner. During the JV term, Kastor (and its personnel) will have had access to Meridian\'s proprietary coating formulations, customer lists, pricing data, and commercial strategy. A 2-year, broad-scope non-compete provides adequate time for the competitive value of this information to decay and for Meridian to consolidate its position post-exit. The geographic limitation to North America reflects Meridian\'s core market and the territory in which Kastor competition would be most damaging.

**[B. Fallback Ladder]{.underline}**

**First Concession --- Duration Reduction to 18 Months**

Cordero has pre-approved movement from 2 years to 18 months. This is Meridian\'s primary concession lever on non-compete and should be the first offer made during the August 11 meeting, ideally at the outset of the discussion to establish a pattern of good-faith flexibility.

**Presentation strategy:** Present the 6-month reduction as a significant concession. Cordero should note that Meridian\'s board authorized a 2-year non-compete and that moving to 18 months required internal discussion and approval. In exchange, Cordero should extract value on IP license-back --- specifically, Kastor\'s acceptance of the sublicensing restrictions on Meridian-contributed IP and the Restricted Competitors schedule for co-developed IP.

**Legal enforceability:** Eighteen months falls squarely within the safe harbor for enforceability of non-compete covenants in both Delaware and Texas. Delaware Chancery has consistently enforced 18-month restrictions in LLC and partnership contexts, and the Texas Business and Commerce Code permits post-termination non-competes of up to two years in connection with the sale of a business or equity interest. The 18-month duration is conservative from an enforceability standpoint and gives Meridian comfortable margin.

**Second Concession --- Scope Narrowing**

If Kastor resists the 18-month duration at full scope, Meridian can offer 18 months with a narrowed scope. The narrowed scope would cover:

> \(a\) All specific product lines developed, manufactured, or sold by MKPoly during the JV term (the \"JV Products\"); and
>
> \(b\) Any products using technology, formulations, or processes that were developed, improved, or materially enhanced during the JV term using JV resources or JV-funded R&D (the \"JV-Developed Technology\").

This scope is broader than Kastor\'s proposed limitation to \"only specific JV product lines\" (which would allow Kastor to compete in adjacent product categories using JV-acquired knowledge) but narrower than Meridian\'s opening position of \"all specialty polymer coatings.\" The inclusion of JV-Developed Technology in the scope prevents the most harmful competitive scenario: Kastor exits the JV, applies JV-developed improvements to its own product lines, and competes with MKPoly (and Meridian) in closely adjacent categories. The exclusion of specialty polymer coatings unrelated to the JV acknowledges that Kastor has legitimate pre-existing businesses in coatings categories that MKPoly does not serve.

**Third Concession --- Geographic Narrowing with Product Scope Trade**

If the parties remain at impasse after the first two concessions, Meridian can offer a geographic narrowing in exchange for product-scope breadth. The structure would be:

> \(a\) **Geography:** The non-compete applies only in territories where MKPoly had active sales (defined as at least \$250,000 in revenue in any 12-month period during the final 36 months of the JV term) at the time of the exiting member\'s departure.
>
> \(b\) **Scope:** Within those territories, the non-compete covers all specialty polymer coatings (i.e., Meridian\'s original full-scope opening position), not just JV Products and JV-Developed Technology.

The logic of this trade is that geographic narrowing reduces Kastor\'s compliance burden by limiting the restriction to markets where MKPoly was actually competing, while product-scope breadth ensures that Meridian is fully protected in those markets. A non-compete that covers only narrow product lines in narrow geographies --- Kastor\'s ideal position --- provides no meaningful protection and is effectively no non-compete at all.

**Walk-Away Floor.** Meridian cannot accept a non-compete with both of the following characteristics:

> \(a\) Duration shorter than twelve (12) months; and
>
> \(b\) Scope limited to only specific JV product lines (excluding JV-Developed Technology) in only active-sales territories.

A non-compete that is both short and narrow provides no meaningful protection against post-exit competition. Kastor could exit the JV and, within 12 months, launch competing products in Meridian\'s core markets using knowledge and relationships acquired during the JV term. If the non-compete falls below this floor, it is not worth the paper it is printed on, and Meridian should insist on strengthening other protective mechanisms (particularly IP license-back restrictions) as compensation.

**[C. Commercial Rationale]{.underline}**

The non-compete is classified as a Tier 3 concession item. It has meaningful commercial value --- it protects Meridian\'s competitive position during the vulnerable post-exit period --- but it is available as trading currency to secure gains on Tier 1 items, particularly IP license-back.

This trading posture is justified by several factors. First, the non-compete is time-limited; its protection decays to zero regardless of the agreed duration. IP license-back restrictions, by contrast, can be perpetual and provide structural, ongoing protection. Second, the non-compete is enforceable only through litigation --- if Kastor violates it, Meridian must bring an action for injunctive relief or damages, which is costly and uncertain. IP license-back restrictions, by contrast, are embedded in contractual licenses that can be structured with self-executing remedies (e.g., automatic license termination upon breach). Third, the non-compete is the provision where Kastor\'s position has the most commercial legitimacy: Kastor is a global chemicals company with pre-existing businesses, and an overly broad non-compete genuinely restricts its operations.

One note of caution regarding California enforceability: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits non-compete covenants, even in commercial contexts. However, this risk is mitigated by several factors: (a) MKPoly is a Delaware LLC, and the LLC Agreement will contain a Delaware choice-of-law provision; (b) MKPoly\'s operations are headquartered in Texas; (c) the LLC/partnership exception recognized by some California courts may apply; and (d) to the extent any enforcement action is necessary, the likely forum is Delaware Chancery or a Texas state court, both of which are favorable jurisdictions. Nevertheless, if Kastor raises California enforceability as an argument, Meridian should not dismiss it entirely but should note that the contractual choice-of-law provision controls and that MKPoly has no California nexus.

**[D. Anticipated Kastor Arguments & Recommended Responses]{.underline}**

**Kastor Argument 1 --- Kastor\'s Pre-Existing Global Business**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor will argue that a broad non-compete in \"all specialty polymer coatings\" across all of North America restricts Kastor\'s existing businesses that are entirely unrelated to MKPoly. Kastor manufactures a range of polymer products globally, and many of its existing product lines (e.g., commodity protective coatings for infrastructure, marine coatings) overlap with the broad category of \"specialty polymer coatings\" but are unrelated to MKPoly\'s advanced industrial and aerospace applications. Kastor will contend that the broad scope unfairly penalizes it for entering the JV and that a more targeted restriction is commercially reasonable.

**Recommended response:** Acknowledge that this is a reasonable concern. Meridian is not seeking to restrict Kastor\'s pre-existing businesses that are unrelated to MKPoly. Offer the scope-narrowing concession in the Second Concession above: limit the non-compete to JV Products and JV-Developed Technology. This targeted scope addresses Kastor\'s legitimate concern --- its unrelated product lines are excluded --- while protecting Meridian against the most damaging competitive scenario: Kastor using JV-acquired knowledge to compete in MKPoly\'s specific product categories.

Cordero should emphasize that the non-compete applies only *post-exit*, not during the JV term. During the JV term, both parties are free to operate their standalone businesses subject to the JVA\'s confidentiality and IP provisions. The non-compete is triggered only if a party departs the venture, and its purpose is to prevent a departing partner --- which has had years of access to JV trade secrets and customer relationships --- from immediately weaponizing that knowledge against the remaining partner.

**Kastor Argument 2 --- Duration**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor proposes a 12-month post-exit non-compete, arguing that 12 months is \"market standard\" and provides adequate protection. Kastor\'s counsel will likely cite general enforceability guidelines suggesting that shorter non-competes are more likely to be upheld and that courts impose the minimum necessary restriction.

**Recommended response:** Twelve months is the minimum enforceable period that provides meaningful protection, but it is not \"market standard\" for JV exits involving high-value IP contributions. The appropriate duration depends on the nature of the information at risk and the time required for its competitive value to decay. Meridian\'s coating formulations are the product of over 15 years of R&D, and the customer relationships at issue are built over multi-year sales cycles. Twelve months is insufficient for the competitive value of this information to decay meaningfully.

Move to 18 months and present it as meeting Kastor significantly more than halfway: Meridian moved from 24 months to 18 months (a 25% reduction), while Kastor is asking for only a 17% reduction from its opening of 12 months. The 18-month position is clearly the midpoint, and Meridian\'s willingness to move there first demonstrates good faith. Reference Comparable Transaction #1 (Section VIII.A), which involved an 18-month non-compete in a comparable specialty chemicals JV, as market support.

**[VI. CONTESTED PROVISION #4 --- EXIT VALUATION METHODOLOGY (Sections 10.03--10.04)]{.underline}**

**[A. Meridian\'s Opening / Preferred Position]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s preferred exit valuation methodology, as set forth in its initial markup of Sections 10.03--10.04, is as follows:

> • **Valuation Metric:** Trailing twelve (12) month EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization), as determined in accordance with GAAP as consistently applied in MKPoly\'s audited financial statements.
>
> • **Multiple:** 6.5× trailing 12-month EBITDA.
>
> • **Appraiser:** Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP, serving in its capacity as MKPoly\'s independent auditor.
>
> • **Trigger Events:** (a) Change of control of either member (defined as a transfer of more than 50% of voting equity in any 12-month period); (b) material breach by either member that remains uncured after the applicable notice and cure period under Section 9.02.
>
> • **Mechanics:** Upon the occurrence of a trigger event, the non-triggering member may deliver a buy-sell notice to the triggering member. The triggering member shall have thirty (30) days to elect to buy or sell at the valuation determined by the appraiser. If the triggering member elects to sell, the non-triggering member shall purchase at the determined valuation. If the triggering member elects to buy, the non-triggering member shall sell at the determined valuation.

**Rationale:** Using MKPoly\'s own auditor as the exit valuation appraiser reduces cost and time and leverages Frazier Whitmore\'s institutional knowledge of MKPoly\'s financials. The 6.5× EBITDA multiple is at the conservative end of the comparable-transaction range identified by Frazier Whitmore (6.2×--8.1×, median 7.1×) and is consistent with Meridian\'s assessment that, at the time of formation, MKPoly is a development-stage venture that may or may not achieve the steady-state profitability necessary to justify a higher multiple.

The use of trailing 12-month EBITDA as the foundational metric is deliberate: EBITDA captures actual operating performance and is not susceptible to the projection-based manipulation that makes discounted cash flow (\"DCF\") and comparable-company analysis less reliable in exit-valuation contexts. Both parties can verify the EBITDA figure against audited financial statements, reducing the scope for dispute.

**[B. Fallback Ladder]{.underline}**

**First Concession --- Multiple Adjustment to 6.8×**

Meridian\'s first concession is to move from 6.5× to 6.8× EBITDA, a 0.3× increase that represents a 4.6% increase in exit valuation. This movement should be presented as reflecting a deeper review of the Frazier Whitmore comparable-transactions data:

> • Comparable Transaction #1: 6.8× EBITDA
>
> • Comparable Transaction #2: 7.4× EBITDA
>
> • Comparable Transaction #3: 7.1× EBITDA
>
> • Range: 6.2×--8.1× (broader Frazier Whitmore dataset)
>
> • Median: 7.1×

At 6.8×, Meridian remains below the median but has moved meaningfully from its opening position. Cordero should reserve the 7.0× concession for the second step, allowing room for a visible further concession if needed.

**Presentation strategy:** Frame the move as data-driven, not pressure-driven. Cordero should say something like: \"Our advisors have done additional work on the comparables, and we\'re comfortable moving to 6.8, which is supported by the data.\" This positions the concession as objective and analytical rather than a negotiation retreat.

**Second Concession --- 7.0× with Hybrid Appraiser Mechanism**

Meridian\'s second concession addresses both the multiple and the appraiser independence concern simultaneously:

> \(a\) **Multiple:** 7.0× trailing 12-month EBITDA. This is Cordero\'s authorized ceiling and is near the 7.1× median of the Frazier Whitmore comparable dataset. Meridian cannot exceed 7.0× without returning to its board.
>
> \(b\) **Hybrid Appraiser Mechanism:** Frazier Whitmore & Co., LLP prepares the initial exit valuation using the agreed methodology (7.0× trailing 12-month EBITDA per audited financial statements). If either party objects to the Frazier Whitmore determination, the objecting party may request an independent review by a jointly selected Big Four accounting firm (Deloitte, EY, KPMG, or PwC). The independent firm shall review the Frazier Whitmore valuation and issue its own determination using the same methodology. If the parties cannot agree on a Big Four firm within fifteen (15) days, the selection shall be made by the AAA Commercial Arbitration panel in Houston.
>
> \(c\) **Cost Allocation:** The cost of the independent review shall be borne entirely by the objecting party unless the independent firm\'s determination deviates from Frazier Whitmore\'s determination by more than 10%, in which case the cost shall be shared equally. This cost-allocation structure discourages frivolous objections while ensuring that genuine disagreements are resolved independently.
>
> \(d\) **Binding Effect:** If an independent review is conducted, the independent firm\'s determination shall be final and binding on both parties.

**Rationale:** This structure preserves Frazier Whitmore\'s institutional role as the first-line valuation appraiser while providing Kastor with the independent review mechanism it has requested. The 10% deviation threshold for cost-sharing is important: it creates a meaningful economic disincentive for a party to object unless it genuinely believes Frazier Whitmore\'s determination is materially inaccurate. In practice, we expect the independent review to be invoked rarely, as Frazier Whitmore\'s valuations --- based on audited financials and a defined methodology --- are likely to be reasonable.

**Third Concession --- Baseball Arbitration with Collar**

If Kastor insists on full appraiser independence and rejects the hybrid mechanism, Meridian can accept Kastor\'s proposed baseball arbitration mechanism, but with critical guardrails:

> \(a\) **Process:** Each party selects one independent appraiser (who must be a nationally recognized valuation firm with experience in specialty chemicals). The two party-selected appraisers jointly select a third independent appraiser. Each appraiser prepares an independent valuation. The third appraiser then selects one of the three valuations (including its own) as the final determination --- i.e., true baseball arbitration, where the final result must be one of the submitted valuations, not an average or compromise.
>
> \(b\) **Methodological Constraint:** All appraisers must use trailing 12-month EBITDA as the foundational valuation metric. No appraiser may use discounted cash flow (DCF), comparable-company analysis, or any other forward-looking methodology as the primary valuation method. EBITDA adjustments must follow GAAP as applied by Frazier Whitmore & Co. in its most recent audit of MKPoly\'s financial statements.
>
> \(c\) **Multiple Collar:** The EBITDA multiple applied by any appraiser must fall within a range of 6.0× to 8.0× (the \"Collar\"). Any valuation that applies a multiple outside the Collar shall be deemed non-compliant and shall be excluded from the baseball arbitration. The Collar prevents outlier results: no appraiser can lowball Meridian at 4× or inflate the valuation at 12×.
>
> \(d\) **Timeline:** The entire process --- from selection of appraisers to final determination --- shall be completed within ninety (90) calendar days.
>
> \(e\) **Cost:** Each party bears the cost of its own appraiser. The cost of the third appraiser is shared equally.

**Rationale:** Baseball arbitration generally produces moderate results because each appraiser has an incentive to submit a reasonable valuation --- an extreme position risks being passed over in favor of a more moderate submission. The methodological constraint (EBITDA only, no DCF) and the Collar (6.0×--8.0×) further narrow the range of possible outcomes. At 6.0×--8.0×, the minimum outcome is a conservative valuation within the comparable range, and the maximum is a premium valuation still supported by market data. Meridian is comfortable with any result in this range.

**Walk-Away Floor.** Meridian cannot accept a valuation methodology that:

> \(a\) Uses DCF or other forward-looking methods as the primary or sole valuation methodology. DCF valuations are inherently susceptible to manipulation of growth-rate assumptions, discount rates, and terminal values. In a disputed exit, the incentive to manipulate projections is extreme, and the resulting valuation disputes are protracted and expensive.
>
> \(b\) Applies an EBITDA multiple below 6.0× or permits unconstrained multiples (no collar). A multiple below 6.0× would undervalue MKPoly relative to every comparable transaction in the Frazier Whitmore dataset, and an unconstrained process creates the risk of outlier results.
>
> \(c\) Excludes trailing 12-month EBITDA as the foundational metric. EBITDA is the most objective, verifiable, and commonly used metric for specialty chemicals valuations. Any departure from EBITDA introduces subjectivity and dispute risk.

Note that Meridian\'s 7.0× ceiling on its own affirmative proposals does not apply if the ultimate valuation mechanism (e.g., baseball arbitration) produces a higher result. If a third-party appraiser determines that MKPoly is worth 7.5× EBITDA, Meridian benefits from the upside.

**[C. Commercial Rationale]{.underline}**

A defined, EBITDA-based exit valuation methodology protects Meridian in several important ways:

**1. IP Value Capture.** Meridian\'s IP contribution (\$28.6M) is embedded in MKPoly\'s operations. At exit, the value of that IP will be reflected in MKPoly\'s EBITDA --- higher-performing coatings generate higher revenue and margins, which flow through to higher EBITDA. An EBITDA-based methodology captures this value automatically, without the need for a separate (and potentially contentious) IP valuation at exit.

**2. Objectivity and Verifiability.** EBITDA is calculated from audited financial statements. Both parties can verify the numerator (EBITDA) independently, and the agreed multiple eliminates the most significant source of valuation dispute (the appropriate capitalization rate). By contrast, DCF requires agreement on growth rates, discount rates, and terminal values --- each of which is subject to wide variation and aggressive assumptions.

**3. Market Validation.** The Frazier Whitmore comparable-transaction dataset validates the 6.5×--7.0× range as conservative but defensible. The 7.1× median provides natural cover for movement to 7.0×, and the 6.2×--8.1× range provides the boundaries for the Collar in the baseball arbitration fallback.

**4. Simplicity.** A defined methodology reduces the cost and time of an exit transaction. If the parties agree on the methodology in advance, the exit valuation can be determined within weeks rather than months, avoiding the operational uncertainty and employee anxiety that accompany protracted valuation disputes.

**[D. Anticipated Kastor Arguments & Recommended Responses]{.underline}**

**Kastor Argument 1 --- Frazier Whitmore Independence**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor\'s Director of Strategic Partnerships, Tomás Escalante, has raised this issue repeatedly in working-team sessions. Kastor\'s position is that Frazier Whitmore, as MKPoly\'s own auditor, has a structural conflict of interest in serving as exit valuation appraiser: the firm\'s ongoing engagement depends on MKPoly\'s continued operations, which may bias its valuation. Kastor\'s counsel has cited AICPA independence standards and will argue that an auditor serving as both the attestation provider and the valuation appraiser creates a \"self-review threat\" to independence.

**Recommended response:** Acknowledge the concern --- it has some technical merit, and dismissing it outright will be counterproductive. However, note that Frazier Whitmore\'s institutional knowledge of MKPoly\'s financials actually benefits both parties: the firm\'s familiarity with MKPoly\'s revenue recognition practices, cost allocation, and accounting policies ensures a more accurate and efficient valuation than a cold-start engagement by an outside firm.

Offer the hybrid appraiser mechanism in the Second Concession: Frazier Whitmore prepares the initial valuation, and either party may request an independent Big Four review if it has concerns about the result. This structure captures the efficiency benefits of using the existing auditor while providing a credible independence backstop. The cost-allocation mechanism (objecting party bears cost unless deviation exceeds 10%) ensures that the independent review is used only when there is a genuine concern about accuracy, not as a delay tactic.

**Kastor Argument 2 --- Multiple Is Too Low**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor will argue that 6.5× undervalues MKPoly, particularly if the JV is performing well at the time of exit. Kastor will cite the 7.1× median of the comparable-transaction range and argue that a venture with Meridian\'s proprietary coating formulations and Kastor\'s European distribution network should command a premium multiple, not a discount.

**Recommended response:** Move to 6.8× in the first concession and 7.0× in the second. Present each move as data-driven and tied to the Frazier Whitmore comparable analysis. At 7.0×, Meridian is near the market center (7.1× median) and can credibly argue that it has conceded to market. If Kastor pushes for a multiple above 7.0×, Cordero should note that she is at her board-authorized ceiling and would need to return for additional authority --- a useful negotiation device that creates a natural break point.

Reference the specific comparables: Comparable Transaction #1 at 6.8× (below Meridian\'s second concession), Comparable Transaction #3 at 7.1× (near Meridian\'s second concession), and Comparable Transaction #2 at 7.4× (above, but in a transaction with different risk characteristics). The weight of the data supports the 6.8×--7.0× range as reasonable and market-appropriate.

**Kastor Argument 3 --- Trigger Events**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor wants to add two additional trigger events beyond Meridian\'s proposed change-of-control and material-breach triggers: (a) deadlock (as defined in the deadlock resolution provisions) and (b) involuntary transfer (including bankruptcy, court-ordered sale, or seizure of a member\'s interest by a creditor or governmental authority).

**Recommended response:** Meridian can accept both additional trigger events. This concession costs Meridian very little and can be presented as a goodwill gesture:

> • **Deadlock trigger:** This is consistent with the tiered deadlock resolution mechanism proposed in Section III. If a deadlock on a Reserved Matter persists after the full escalation process (15-day negotiation + 30-day mediation), either party can initiate the buy-sell mechanism. Adding deadlock as a formal trigger event under Sections 10.03--10.04 simply codifies this linkage.
>
> • **Involuntary transfer trigger:** It is in both parties\' interest to have a buyout mechanism in the event that one member enters bankruptcy or has its interest seized by a creditor. Without this trigger, a member\'s JV interest could end up in the hands of a lender, bankruptcy trustee, or hostile third party --- an outcome neither party wants.

However, Meridian should insist on appropriate carve-outs for intra-group restructurings. Specifically, neither of the following should constitute an involuntary transfer trigger: (a) a reorganization of Kastor\'s corporate structure (e.g., transfer of Kastor\'s JV interest to a wholly owned subsidiary) that does not change the ultimate beneficial ownership or control of Kastor\'s interest; or (b) a merger, consolidation, or similar transaction involving a member\'s parent company, provided that the surviving entity assumes all of the member\'s obligations under the JVA. These carve-outs protect Meridian as well as Kastor --- Meridian\'s own corporate structure may evolve, and it should not be penalized for internal reorganizations.

**[VII. CONTESTED PROVISION #5 --- IP LICENSE-BACK (Section 12.05)]{.underline}**

**THIS IS MERIDIAN\'S HIGHEST-PRIORITY CONTESTED PROVISION** **CORDERO\'S RED LINE --- NO FLEXIBILITY ON CONTRIBUTED IP SUBLICENSING**

**[A. Meridian\'s Opening / Preferred Position]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s preferred IP license-back structure, as set forth in its initial markup of Section 12.05, is as follows:

**1. Contributed IP Reversion.** Upon exit or dissolution of MKPoly, all contributed IP reverts to the contributing party. This principle is agreed by both sides and is not contested. Specifically:

> \(a\) Meridian\'s 23 contributed IP items (as listed on Schedule 12.01), valued at \$28.6M per the Frazier Whitmore appraisal, revert to Meridian.
>
> \(b\) Kastor\'s 9 contributed IP items (as listed on Schedule 12.01(b)), consisting primarily of manufacturing process documentation and certain polymer precursor specifications, revert to Kastor.

**2. Co-Developed IP --- License to Meridian.** Meridian receives an exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual, irrevocable license to all co-developed IP (as defined in Section 12.02 and listed on Schedule 12.02, to be populated during the JV term) for use in North America and any other territory where Meridian has active operations at the time of exit. \"Active operations\" means any territory in which Meridian or its affiliates generated at least \$500,000 in revenue from specialty polymer coatings in the 12 months preceding exit. The exclusive license means Kastor may not license the same co-developed IP in these territories to any third party.

**3. Co-Developed IP --- License to Kastor.** Kastor receives a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual, irrevocable license to all co-developed IP, but with no sublicensing rights whatsoever. Kastor may use the co-developed IP in its own internal operations but may not license, sublicense, transfer, or otherwise make available the co-developed IP to any third party.

**4.
