No License of Contributed IP Post-Exit.** No license of any kind --- exclusive, non-exclusive, or otherwise --- is granted to Kastor with respect to any of Meridian\'s 23 contributed IP items after exit. These are Meridian\'s proprietary assets, and their value is preserved only if they remain exclusively in Meridian\'s control.

**5. Kastor Contributed IP --- License to Meridian.** Kastor\'s 9 contributed IP items revert to Kastor upon exit, but Meridian receives a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to use those items in the continued manufacture and sale of products developed during the JV term. This license ensures that Meridian can continue to produce MKPoly-legacy products without interruption.

**Rationale:** This structure reflects the fundamental asymmetry between the parties\' IP contributions. Meridian\'s contributed IP (\$28.6M, comprising proprietary coating formulations that are the JV\'s core competitive differentiator) is categorically different from Kastor\'s contributed IP (primarily manufacturing process documentation, not independently appraised, and estimated at significantly less than Meridian\'s contribution in commercial value). The license-back structure must reflect this asymmetry.

Meridian\'s proprietary coating formulations --- the CR-7, CR-9, and related systems --- represent the accumulated output of over 15 years of R&D investment. These formulations are protected by a combination of patents, trade secrets, and proprietary know-how. They cannot be replicated through independent R&D in any commercially reasonable timeframe (Kastor\'s own internal estimate, obtained through due diligence, suggests a 3--5 year R&D cycle to develop comparable formulations, with no guarantee of equivalent performance). Post-exit licensing of these formulations to Kastor --- or, worse, Kastor\'s sublicensing to Meridian\'s North American competitors --- would destroy the competitive moat that these formulations provide and would effectively transfer the value of Meridian\'s \$28.6M IP contribution to Kastor and its licensees.

This is Cordero\'s stated red line, and it is fully justified by the commercial realities.

**[B. Fallback Ladder]{.underline}**

**First Concession --- Limited Kastor License to Co-Developed IP Outside North America**

Meridian maintains all positions on contributed IP (no change) but offers Kastor a broader license to co-developed IP than in Meridian\'s opening position:

> \(a\) Kastor receives a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual, irrevocable license to all co-developed IP (per Schedule 12.02) for use in territories outside North America (i.e., Europe, Asia, Africa, South America, Oceania, and the Middle East).
>
> \(b\) Within these territories, Kastor may sublicense co-developed IP, but only to entities that are not listed on Schedule 12.05 (the \"Restricted Competitors\" schedule). Schedule 12.05 shall identify Meridian\'s top 15--20 North American competitors in specialty polymer coatings, together with their controlled affiliates (defined as entities in which a Restricted Competitor owns, directly or indirectly, 50% or more of voting equity). See Appendix B for a proposed draft of Schedule 12.05.
>
> \(c\) Meridian retains its exclusive license to co-developed IP in North America and any other territory where Meridian has active operations.

**Rationale:** This concession gives Kastor the broader co-developed IP license-back that Kastor has identified as its top priority. Kastor can use and sublicense co-developed IP globally --- outside North America --- to any party except Meridian\'s identified competitors. This is a meaningful and valuable concession: it unlocks the full commercial value of co-developed IP for Kastor\'s European and Asian operations, which is Kastor\'s stated interest.

At the same time, the concession preserves Meridian\'s core protection: no sublicensing to Restricted Competitors, regardless of territory. The Restricted Competitors schedule should be drafted broadly enough to capture not just direct competitors but also their affiliates and, where possible, entities that could serve as intermediaries or conduits. See Appendix B for a proposed drafting framework.

**Second Concession --- Reciprocal Territorial Sublicensing with Negative Covenant**

If Kastor insists on sublicensing rights --- arguing that the Restricted Competitors limitation in the First Concession is too broad or too difficult to administer --- Meridian can offer a more structured sublicensing framework:

> \(a\) **Kastor\'s Sublicensing Territory:** Kastor may sublicense co-developed IP only in Europe and Asia (defined as the member states of the European Union, the European Free Trade Association, the United Kingdom, Turkey, and all countries in Asia east of the Ural Mountains and south of the Arctic Circle).
>
> \(b\) **Meridian\'s Sublicensing Territory:** Meridian may sublicense co-developed IP only in the Americas (defined as North America, Central America, South America, and the Caribbean).
>
> \(c\) **Restricted Competitors Covenant:** Neither party may sublicense co-developed IP to any entity listed on Schedule 12.05 (Restricted Competitors), regardless of territory. This prohibition applies even if the sublicense would be exercised outside the Restricted Competitor\'s home territory.
>
> \(d\) **Revenue-Based Negative Covenant:** In addition to the Restricted Competitors list, Kastor covenants not to sublicense co-developed IP to any entity that derives more than 25% of its consolidated annual revenue from the manufacture, sale, or distribution of specialty polymer coatings in North America. This covenant operates as a catch-all: even if a competitor is not named on Schedule 12.05 (perhaps because it was formed after the schedule was finalized), it is captured by the revenue-based test if its North American specialty coatings revenue exceeds 25% of its total revenue.
>
> \(e\) **Audit Right:** Meridian shall have the right, upon reasonable notice and at its own expense, to audit Kastor\'s sublicensing records to verify compliance with the territorial restrictions and the Restricted Competitors covenant.

**Rationale:** This structure technically concedes sublicensing rights to Kastor while using three overlapping mechanisms --- territorial limitation, named Restricted Competitors, and the 25% revenue-based negative covenant --- to prevent the outcome Cordero has identified as a red line. The practical effect is that Kastor can sublicense to European and Asian chemicals companies that are not in the North American specialty coatings business. This covers the vast majority of Kastor\'s potential sublicensees and gives Kastor meaningful commercial flexibility.

The 25% revenue-based negative covenant is a critical innovation. It serves as a dynamic anti-circumvention mechanism: as the North American specialty coatings market evolves and new competitors emerge, the covenant automatically captures any sublicensee with material North American coatings exposure, even if that entity was not identified on Schedule 12.05. This addresses one of Meridian\'s key vulnerabilities --- the risk that the Restricted Competitors list becomes stale over time as new entrants emerge and existing competitors restructure.

**Third Concession --- Improvements Allocation**

Both sides have flagged the improvements gap in prior working-team sessions. The improvements gap refers to IP improvements (i.e., enhancements, modifications, optimizations, or derivative works) made to contributed IP during the JV term using JV resources. The parties have not yet addressed how these improvements are allocated upon exit. Meridian\'s third concession addresses this gap as follows:

> \(a\) **Improvements to Meridian-Contributed IP:** Improvements to Meridian\'s 23 contributed IP items made during the JV term using JV resources shall revert to Meridian upon exit. However, Kastor shall receive a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to those improvements for use outside North America, subject to the same sublicensing restrictions applicable to co-developed IP under the First Concession or Second Concession above (i.e., no sublicensing to Restricted Competitors or entities exceeding the 25% revenue threshold).
>
> \(b\) **Improvements to Kastor-Contributed IP:** Improvements to Kastor\'s 9 contributed IP items (primarily manufacturing process documentation) made during the JV term using JV resources shall revert to Kastor upon exit. However, Meridian shall receive a non-exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to those improvements for use in the Americas.
>
> \(c\) **Classification of Improvements:** For the avoidance of doubt, an improvement to contributed IP shall be classified as an improvement to the contributing party\'s IP only if the improvement is a derivative work that builds primarily upon the contributing party\'s underlying IP. Improvements that build equally upon both parties\' contributed IP, or that are not primarily derivative of either party\'s contributed IP, shall be classified as co-developed IP and treated accordingly under the co-developed IP provisions above.

**Rationale:** This concession addresses the improvements gap symmetrically and gives Kastor meaningful value on an issue that Meridian has acknowledged is unresolved. Kastor has contributed manufacturing process documentation to the JV, and improvements to that documentation made using JV resources (including Kastor-funded capital) should be available to both parties post-exit. The reciprocal structure --- each party gets improvements to its own contributed IP back, with a license to the other party in defined territories --- is commercially fair and easy to administer.

The classification provision (subsection (c)) is important: it prevents disputes about whether a particular innovation is an \"improvement\" to contributed IP or independent \"co-developed\" IP. The \"primarily derivative\" test provides a clear classification standard, and innovations that don\'t clearly derive from one party\'s contributed IP default into the co-developed IP category, which has its own licensing rules.

**Walk-Away Floor.** Meridian\'s absolute positions on IP license-back are as follows. **These positions are non-negotiable and cannot be traded for concessions on any other provision:**

> \(a\) **No sublicensing by Kastor of Meridian-contributed IP, under any circumstances.** The 23 contributed IP items listed on Schedule 12.01 are Meridian\'s proprietary technology. No license --- exclusive, non-exclusive, limited, or otherwise --- shall be granted to Kastor for these items post-exit. No sublicensing, direct or indirect, shall be permitted. This prohibition is absolute and applies regardless of territory, regardless of the identity of the sublicensee, and regardless of any commercial justification offered by Kastor.
>
> \(b\) **No sublicensing of co-developed IP by Kastor to any entity on Schedule 12.05 (Restricted Competitors), regardless of territory.** Even if the sublicense would be exercised in Europe or Asia, a sublicense to a Restricted Competitor creates the risk that the competitor will use the co-developed IP in its North American operations. The Restricted Competitors prohibition must be global, not territorial.
>
> \(c\) **No exclusive license to Kastor on any co-developed IP, in any territory.** Exclusivity in Kastor\'s favor would prevent Meridian from using co-developed IP in the territory covered by the exclusive license. Since co-developed IP incorporates elements of Meridian\'s contributed IP, exclusivity in Kastor\'s favor would effectively restrict Meridian\'s use of its own technology --- an unacceptable result.

Cordero\'s instruction is absolute: **\"No structure that allows Kastor to put our proprietary coating formulations in the hands of our North American competitors.\"** Any proposal from Kastor that creates a pathway --- direct or indirect, immediate or contingent --- for Meridian\'s CR-7 and CR-9 formulations (or co-developed improvements thereto) to reach Polaris Coatings Corp., Endurance Surface Technologies, Trident Polymer Systems, or any other Schedule 12.05 Restricted Competitor must be rejected without further discussion.

**[C. Commercial Rationale]{.underline}**

**1. Value of Contributed IP.** Meridian\'s 23 contributed IP items were independently appraised at \$28.6M by Frazier Whitmore & Co. This valuation reflects the items\' replacement cost, income-generating capacity, and market exclusivity. The formulations are the product of over 15 years of R&D investment totaling approximately \$75M in cumulative spending. Post-exit licensing to Kastor would transfer this value without compensation; post-exit sublicensing to Kastor\'s sublicensees would multiply the transfer. The IP license-back provisions are the mechanism by which Meridian protects its \$28.6M contribution against value leakage.

**2. Improvements Gap Risk.** During the JV term, MKPoly will invest in R&D that improves upon Meridian\'s contributed formulations. These improvements --- which will be funded in part by Kastor\'s capital contributions --- are derivative works that build on Meridian\'s underlying technology. If the improvements are classified as co-developed IP (rather than as improvements to contributed IP), they may be subject to broader licensing and sublicensing than Meridian\'s contributed IP itself. The improvements allocation in Third Concession is designed to close this gap by treating improvements to contributed IP as the contributing party\'s IP for licensing purposes.

**3. Kastor\'s Contributed IP Asymmetry.** Kastor\'s 9 contributed IP items are primarily manufacturing process documentation --- useful but not competitively unique. These items were not independently appraised, and their commercial value is estimated at significantly less than Meridian\'s contributed IP. The asymmetry between the parties\' IP contributions justifies asymmetric license-back terms: Meridian\'s more valuable and more competitively sensitive IP warrants stronger protections.

**4. North American Market Concentration.** The North American specialty polymer coatings market is concentrated among approximately 20 major players. Market share is difficult to gain and easy to lose. Sublicensing of Meridian\'s proprietary formulations to even one major competitor could result in market-share loss that would take years to recover, if recovery is possible at all. The risk is not hypothetical: Polaris Coatings Corp. has publicly stated its intention to develop advanced coating systems competitive with Meridian\'s CR-7, and access to Meridian\'s formulations through a Kastor sublicense would accelerate Polaris\'s timeline by an estimated 3--5 years.

**[D. Anticipated Kastor Arguments & Recommended Responses]{.underline}**

**Kastor Argument 1 --- Kastor Funded IP Improvements**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor will argue that its capital contributions (€38M in cash, plus ongoing funding of JV operations) will fund improvements to Meridian-contributed IP during the JV term. Kastor will contend that these improvements are, at least in part, Kastor-funded, and that Kastor should therefore have the right to license and sublicense them freely. Kastor may frame this as a \"return on investment\" argument: Kastor\'s cash funded the improvements, and Kastor is entitled to the commercial benefits of that investment.

**Recommended response:** Offer the Third Concession (improvements allocation with territorial and sublicensing restrictions). Acknowledge that Kastor\'s capital contributions will fund JV R&D, including improvements to Meridian-contributed IP, and that Kastor is entitled to a return on that investment. However, emphasize two critical points:

First, the underlying IP remains Meridian\'s proprietary technology. Improvements to Meridian-contributed IP are derivative works --- they derive their value from the contributed base IP. Without Meridian\'s CR-7 formulation, an improvement to CR-7 has no independent commercial value. Kastor\'s capital funded the improvement, but Meridian\'s technology made the improvement possible. The improvements allocation reflects this reality by reverting improvements to the contributing party while granting the other party a non-exclusive territorial license.

Second, Kastor\'s return on its capital investment comes primarily through its share of MKPoly\'s operating profits during the JV term and its share of exit proceeds. The IP license-back provisions are separate from the economic returns of the JV --- they govern what happens to the technology after the partnership ends. Kastor\'s economic return is captured through equity, not through IP licensing.

**Kastor Argument 2 --- Sublicensing Is Essential for Kastor\'s Global Business**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor\'s top priority in the IP license-back negotiation is a broad co-developed IP license with sublicensing rights. Kastor will argue that it needs sublicensing to monetize JV technology in its global chemicals business --- specifically, to incorporate co-developed formulations into products sold through Kastor\'s European and Asian distribution networks. Kastor may present this as a deal-level issue: \"Without sublicensing, our board will not approve the investment.\"

**Recommended response:** Offer the First and Second Concessions on co-developed IP. Emphasize that Meridian is offering a broad, valuable license: Kastor can use co-developed IP worldwide, and in Europe and Asia, Kastor can sublicense to any entity except Meridian\'s named North American competitors. The only restriction is sublicensing to entities on Schedule 12.05 --- a list of 15--20 companies (and their affiliates) that are Meridian\'s direct competitors in North America. Kastor\'s global business outside North America is unrestricted by this provision.

If Kastor presses, Cordero should ask a clarifying question: \"Which of your potential sublicensees would be excluded by our Restricted Competitors list?\" If Kastor cannot identify a specific, commercially important sublicensee that appears on Schedule 12.05, its objection is theoretical rather than practical, and Meridian can hold its position. If Kastor does identify such a sublicensee, Meridian should evaluate whether that entity can be removed from Schedule 12.05 without compromising Meridian\'s competitive position --- but only if the entity genuinely does not compete with Meridian in North America.

**Kastor Argument 3 --- Symmetry**

**Anticipated argument:** Kastor\'s counsel, Tomás Escalante, will argue that any sublicensing restriction on Kastor should apply equally to Meridian. If Kastor cannot sublicense to Meridian\'s competitors, then Meridian should not be able to sublicense to Kastor\'s competitors. The principle of \"equal treatment for equal partners\" is a frequent Kastor theme.

**Recommended response:** Accept symmetry on co-developed IP sublicensing. The Second Concession already structures this symmetrically: Kastor can sublicense in Europe and Asia, Meridian can sublicense in the Americas, and neither party can sublicense to the other\'s listed competitors. This reciprocal structure is easy to administer and gives Kastor the \"equal treatment\" it seeks.

However, reject symmetry on contributed IP. The parties\' contributed IP items are different in nature (Meridian\'s proprietary coating formulations vs. Kastor\'s manufacturing process documentation), different in independently appraised value (\$28.6M vs. not appraised), and different in competitive sensitivity (Meridian\'s formulations are the JV\'s core differentiator; Kastor\'s process documentation is operationally important but not competitively unique). Symmetric treatment of fundamentally asymmetric contributions is not \"equal\" --- it is inequitable.

Cordero should frame this clearly: \"Symmetry applies when the underlying assets are comparable. Our coating formulations are not comparable to your process documentation --- they are worth 14 times more, they are the reason this JV exists, and they require different protections. We have offered you a very broad license to co-developed IP on symmetric terms. What we cannot do is treat our contributed IP the same as yours when the contributions are not the same.\"

**[VIII. COMPARABLE TRANSACTION BENCHMARKS]{.underline}**

The following comparable-transaction summaries are drawn from the Frazier Whitmore & Co. specialty chemicals transaction database and from publicly available information supplemented by our firm\'s deal experience. All three transactions involve formation of specialty chemicals JVs with structures comparable to MKPoly. Confidential details have been omitted where required by third-party confidentiality obligations.

**[A. Comparable Transaction #1 --- Specialty Coatings JV (2023)]{.underline}**

**Transaction Overview:** Formation of a specialty coatings JV between a U.S.-based coatings manufacturer and a European chemicals company. The JV was focused on advanced protective coatings for energy-sector applications. The U.S. partner contributed proprietary coating technology and customer relationships; the European partner contributed capital and access to international markets.

**Key Terms:**

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Provision**                       **Terms**
  ----------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Equity Split                        60% (U.S. partner) / 40% (European partner)

  Exit EBITDA Multiple                6.8× trailing 12-month EBITDA

  Non-Compete                         18 months post-exit, covering JV product lines and closely adjacent products, geographic scope limited to North America and Western Europe

  Board Composition                   3-2 (majority partner appoints 3), with minority protective rights on 5 specified Reserved Matters (amendment, dissolution, asset sale, capex \>\$3M, new members)

  Capital Call Cure                   45-day cure period, uncapped dilution upon expiration

  IP License-Back                     Contributed IP exclusive license-back to contributing party upon dissolution; no sublicensing by either party of contributed IP; co-developed IP licensed non-exclusively to both parties with no sublicensing
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Relevance to MKPoly:** This transaction is the closest comparable to MKPoly in terms of equity structure (60/40 vs. 55/45), technology contribution profile (one partner contributing proprietary coatings IP), and industry segment (specialty coatings). The terms on every key dimension --- 3-2 board, 6.8× multiple, 18-month non-compete, 45-day cure, no contributed IP sublicensing --- support Meridian\'s positions or fall within Meridian\'s concession range.

**[B. Comparable Transaction #2 --- Advanced Materials JV (2024)]{.underline}**

**Transaction Overview:** Formation of an advanced materials JV between a U.S. specialty chemicals company and a Japanese materials conglomerate. The JV was focused on high-performance polymer composites for automotive and electronics applications. The U.S. partner contributed formulation technology and North American manufacturing capacity; the Japanese partner contributed capital, Asian market access, and manufacturing process expertise.

**Key Terms:**

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Provision**                       **Terms**
  ----------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Equity Split                        55% (U.S. partner) / 45% (Japanese partner)

  Exit EBITDA Multiple                7.4× trailing 12-month EBITDA

  Non-Compete                         24 months post-exit, broad scope (all polymer composites), North America and Asia

  Board Composition                   3-3 (equal representation), majority-appointed chairperson with casting vote on non-Reserved Matters; majority partner retains sole right to appoint CEO and CFO

  Capital Call Cure                   60-day cure period; dilution capped at 15% per annum with automatic forced-sale mechanism if dilution exceeds 15% in any 24-month period

  IP License-Back                     Contributed IP reverts to contributing party; co-developed IP licensed non-exclusively to both parties with territorial sublicensing restrictions (U.S. partner: Americas; Japanese partner: Asia-Pacific); mutual sublicensing restrictions preventing licensing to named competitors
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Relevance to MKPoly:** This transaction has the identical equity structure to MKPoly (55/45) and involves similar contribution dynamics (technology + capital). Notably, even with a 3-3 board, the majority partner retained a casting vote and sole officer-appointment authority --- precisely Meridian\'s Second Concession position on governance. The 7.4× multiple is above Meridian\'s authorized ceiling but validates the upper end of the comparable range. The territorial sublicensing structure for co-developed IP mirrors Meridian\'s Second Concession on IP license-back.

**[C. Comparable Transaction #3 --- Polymer Manufacturing JV (2024)]{.underline}**

**Transaction Overview:** Formation of a polymer manufacturing JV between two global chemicals companies with equal equity contributions. The JV was focused on commodity and specialty polymer production for packaging and industrial applications. Both partners contributed significant capital; one partner contributed proprietary polymerization technology, and the other contributed feedstock supply agreements and manufacturing infrastructure.

**Key Terms:**

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Provision**                       **Terms**
  ----------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Equity Split                        50% / 50%

  Exit EBITDA Multiple                7.1× trailing 12-month EBITDA, determined by baseball arbitration (each party selects one appraiser, those two select a third; third appraiser\'s determination is binding)

  Non-Compete                         12 months post-exit, narrow scope (specific JV product lines only), geography limited to territories with active JV sales

  Board Composition                   Equal board (3-3), with mediation-then-forced-buyout deadlock mechanism; no casting vote

  Capital Call Cure                   60-day cure period, uncapped dilution

  IP License-Back                     Co-developed IP licensed exclusively to JV during term; upon dissolution, non-exclusively to both parties with mutual sublicensing restrictions (neither party may sublicense to named competitors of the other)
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Relevance to MKPoly:** This transaction is less directly comparable because of the 50/50 equity split (MKPoly is 55/45), which explains the equal board without a casting vote. However, the 7.1× EBITDA multiple (determined by baseball arbitration) validates the market center and supports Meridian\'s movement to 7.0×. The mutual sublicensing restrictions on co-developed IP confirm that sublicensing protections are standard in specialty chemicals JVs, even between equal partners.

**[D. Takeaways for Negotiation]{.underline}**

The three comparable transactions, taken together, provide strong empirical support for Meridian\'s positions on each contested provision:

**Non-Compete Duration:** An 18-month non-compete is squarely within market range. Comparable #1 used 18 months, Comparable #2 used 24 months, and Comparable #3 used 12 months. The median is 18 months, which is precisely Meridian\'s first concession position. Cordero should cite this data when presenting the 18-month offer.

**Exit Valuation Multiple:** The comparable range is 6.8×--7.4×, with a median of 7.1×. Meridian\'s 6.5× opening is below the range; Meridian\'s 7.0× authorized ceiling is near the median. Movement to 7.0× is clearly market-appropriate and can be presented as conceding to market center.

**Board Composition:** In both 60/40 and 55/45 transactions (Comparables #1 and #2), the majority partner retained governance control --- either through a 3-2 board (Comparable #1) or through a 3-3 board with casting vote and sole officer-appointment authority (Comparable #2). Only the 50/50 transaction (Comparable #3) used an equal board without a casting vote. This confirms that Meridian\'s 55% equity position justifies either a 3-2 board or, at minimum, a 3-3 board with structural control mechanisms.

**Sublicensing Restrictions:** Contributed IP sublicensing restrictions are universal in comparable transactions. No comparable transaction permitted sublicensing of contributed IP. Co-developed IP sublicensing restrictions (territorial limitations, named-competitor prohibitions) appeared in two of three transactions (Comparables #2 and #3). Meridian\'s positions on IP license-back are firmly within market norms.

**Capital Call Cure Period:** Cure periods of 45--60 days are standard. No comparable transaction used a cure period shorter than 45 days or longer than 60 days. Meridian\'s second concession (60 days with penalty interest) is at the top of the market range but is supported by the data.

**[IX. CROSS-PROVISION NEGOTIATION STRATEGY & SEQUENCING]{.underline}**

**[A. Recommended Sequencing for August 11 Meeting]{.underline}**

The order in which provisions are discussed at the principals meeting will significantly influence outcomes. Negotiations that begin with high-stakes, deeply contested items risk early impasse and adversarial dynamics that poison subsequent discussions. Negotiations that begin with areas of potential agreement build momentum, establish trust, and create a pattern of reciprocal concession that makes later agreement on harder issues more likely.

**Recommended order of discussion:**

**1. Non-Compete (Section 11.01) --- Start Here.** Open with the non-compete because it is Meridian\'s Tier 3 item with the greatest concession room and the most visible movement. Cordero should lead by offering to reduce the non-compete from 2 years to 18 months --- a concession Kastor has identified as important and that Cordero has pre-approved. This sets a cooperative tone and establishes Meridian as a willing, flexible negotiation partner. It also creates a clear credit: Kastor now owes a reciprocal concession.

**2. Exit Valuation (Sections 10.03--10.04) --- Build Momentum.** Proceed to exit valuation, another Tier 3 item. Offer movement from 6.5× to 6.8× (or, if the non-compete concession generated significant goodwill, to 7.0× immediately). Present the movement as data-driven. If Kastor raises the appraiser-independence issue, offer the hybrid mechanism or baseball arbitration with collar. By the end of this discussion, Meridian should have offered two visible concessions, and Kastor should feel that the negotiation is productive and that Meridian is acting in good faith.

**3. IP License-Back (Section 12.05) --- Highest Priority, Negotiate from Strength.** With two Tier 3 concessions on the table, Cordero is now in the strongest possible position to hold firm on IP license-back. The message is: \"We\'ve moved significantly on non-compete and valuation. On IP licensing, we have a red line that I need to explain.\" Present Meridian\'s opening position on contributed IP (no license to Kastor, no sublicensing under any circumstances) and offer the First Concession on co-developed IP (non-exclusive license to Kastor outside North America with Restricted Competitors restrictions). Frame the co-developed IP concession as responsive to Kastor\'s stated top priority while making clear that contributed IP is non-negotiable.

**4. Management Control (Section 5.01) --- Frame as Partnership, Not Control.** After the IP license-back discussion, turn to governance. By this point, Cordero has demonstrated flexibility on two items and firmness on one, which establishes credibility. Present the 3-2 board with narrow Reserved Matters as the preferred position. If Kastor pushes back, offer the 3-3 board with casting vote as the final fallback. Link governance to IP: \"Our board composition reflects our need to protect the technology we\'ve brought to this partnership. If we have operational control, we\'re comfortable with the co-developed IP licensing framework we just discussed.\"

**5. Capital Calls (Section 4.03) --- Close on Middle Ground.** Address capital calls last. This is a Tier 2 item where both sides have room to move, and it provides a natural closing discussion where both parties can claim concessions. Offer the 60-day cure period with penalty interest. If Kastor insists on a loan alternative, offer the punitive-terms loan. End the meeting on a constructive note with an item that can be resolved through give-and-take.

**[B. Package Deal Proposals]{.underline}**

If the item-by-item discussion does not produce resolution, Cordero may need to present a package deal --- a comprehensive proposal that addresses all five provisions simultaneously. Package proposals force trade-offs and prevent the parties from optimizing each provision independently (which tends to produce deadlock, as each party maximizes its position on each item without considering the overall picture).

Two alternative packages are prepared below. Package A is Meridian\'s preferred outcome; Package B is the final fallback.

Package B should be presented only if Package A is rejected and only if Kastor reciprocates with acceptance of the IP license-back framework.

**[C. Linkage Strategy]{.underline}**

The following instructions are for Cordero\'s use during the negotiation:

**1. Link IP concessions to governance gains.** If Kastor asks for concessions on IP license-back (e.g., broader sublicensing rights, weaker Restricted Competitors protections), require reciprocal concessions on management control. The specific language Cordero can use: \"We\'re open to discussing broader co-developed IP licensing, but that discussion only works for us if governance reflects our majority position. If you want more flexibility on IP, we need the 3-2 board.\" This linkage is logical: broader IP licensing increases Meridian\'s risk, and governance control is the mechanism by which Meridian manages that risk.

**2. Never concede on both Tier 1 items simultaneously.** If Cordero moves on governance (from 3-2 to 3-3 with casting vote), she must not simultaneously concede on IP licensing (e.g., by weakening the Restricted Competitors covenant or the revenue-based negative covenant). Each Tier 1 concession must be funded by a Kastor concession on the other Tier 1 item or on a Tier 2/3 item of equivalent value.

**3. Use Tier 3 concessions proactively.** Do not wait for Kastor to ask for non-compete or valuation concessions. Offer them proactively and early, establishing the pattern of reciprocity before the conversation turns to Tier 1 items. A concession offered freely is worth more in negotiation goodwill than the same concession extracted under pressure.

**4. Avoid incremental negotiation on IP.** The IP license-back provisions should be presented as a package (opening position plus available concessions), not negotiated incrementally. Incremental negotiation creates the impression that every element of Meridian\'s IP position is movable, inviting Kastor to push on each element individually. Present the co-developed IP licensing framework as a carefully structured package where the territorial restrictions, Restricted Competitors schedule, and revenue-based negative covenant work together as a system. Removing any one element undermines the whole.

**5. If Kastor presents a package, evaluate holistically.** Do not respond to each element of a Kastor package individually. Take the full package under advisement, request a break if necessary, and evaluate the aggregate effect before responding. This prevents the negotiation from devolving into a point-by-point critique that produces adversarial dynamics.

**[X. WALK-AWAY ANALYSIS]{.underline}**

**[A. Deal-Breaker Scenarios]{.underline}**

There are three scenarios under which Meridian should terminate negotiations and decline to proceed with the MKPoly formation:

**Scenario 1 --- Kastor Sublicensing of Contributed IP.** If Kastor obtains any sublicensing right --- direct or indirect, limited or unlimited, territorial or global --- over any of Meridian\'s 23 contributed IP items (\$28.6M in appraised value), Meridian should walk away. The contributed IP is Meridian\'s core competitive asset. Sublicensing to any third party, whether a named competitor or not, creates an uncontrollable dissemination risk. Once a sublicense is granted, Meridian cannot control how the sublicensee uses the technology, whether the sublicensee further disseminates it, or whether the technology eventually reaches Meridian\'s competitors through secondary transfers, personnel movement, or reverse engineering. This risk is existential and justifies deal termination.

**Scenario 2 --- Loss of Effective Management Control.** If the final governance structure gives Kastor an effective veto over ordinary-course business decisions (e.g., through broad supermajority requirements, equal board without casting vote, or joint CEO appointment) and Meridian has no casting vote or equivalent tiebreak mechanism, Meridian should walk away. A governance structure in which a 45% partner can block operating decisions --- pricing, product development, key hires, customer strategy --- is not a joint venture; it is a hostage situation. Meridian\'s 55% equity position was negotiated to provide operational control, and a governance structure that nullifies this control is inconsistent with the economic terms of the deal.

**Scenario 3 --- Cumulative Concession Overload.** Even if no single red line is crossed, a combination of concessions across all five provisions that, taken together, leaves Meridian with majority equity but minority effective control is a deal-breaker. For example: a 3-3 board with broad supermajority requirements (losing management control), a 10% dilution cap with market-rate loan alternative (losing capital call leverage), a 12-month narrow non-compete (losing competitive protection), and broad co-developed IP sublicensing with weak Restricted Competitors protections (weakening IP protection) --- even if contributed IP sublicensing is prohibited --- would collectively leave Meridian in a position worse than its BATNA. Cordero should evaluate any package proposal against this cumulative standard.

**[B. BATNA Assessment]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s best alternative to a negotiated agreement with Kastor is to proceed independently with its proprietary coating formulations, without the JV. This alternative is suboptimal but viable:

**What Meridian Retains Without the JV:**

> • Full ownership and control of all 23 contributed IP items (\$28.6M value), including the CR-7 and CR-9 coating systems
>
> • All existing customer relationships in North American specialty coatings
>
> • All existing manufacturing infrastructure at the Houston campus
>
> • \$14.3M in cash that would have been contributed to MKPoly, available for redeployment

**What Meridian Loses Without the JV:**

> • Access to Kastor\'s manufacturing process documentation (9 contributed IP items), which Meridian would need to develop or acquire independently. Estimated timeline: 18--24 months. Estimated cost: \$15M--\$20M in capital expenditure, comprising facility modifications, process engineering, and personnel recruitment.
>
> • Access to Kastor\'s European distribution network, which would have provided MKPoly with an established channel for international sales. Meridian would need to build its own European distribution capability or partner with a different distribution partner. Estimated timeline: 12--18 months. Estimated cost: \$5M--\$8M.
>
> • Kastor\'s cash contribution (\$41.8M), which would have funded the majority of MKPoly\'s initial operations. Without this funding, Meridian would need to finance independently or seek alternative financing. Meridian\'s current revolving credit facility provides up to \$50M in available capacity, which is sufficient but would increase Meridian\'s leverage ratio.

**Net Assessment:** The JV remains preferable to independent development --- it accelerates Meridian\'s entry into advanced polymer manufacturing by approximately 18--24 months and provides access to Kastor\'s capital and distribution capabilities. However, the JV is not essential to Meridian\'s core business. Meridian can succeed independently, and the walk-away option is credible. This credibility strengthens Meridian\'s negotiating position.

**[C. Kastor\'s BATNA Assessment]{.underline}**

Understanding Kastor\'s alternatives is essential to assessing Meridian\'s leverage. Our analysis, based on publicly available information and intelligence gathered during due diligence, suggests that Kastor\'s BATNA is significantly weaker than Meridian\'s:

**What Kastor Lacks Without the JV:**

> • Proprietary coating formulations. Kastor does not have coating technology comparable to Meridian\'s CR-7 and CR-9 systems. Kastor\'s existing coatings product line consists of commodity-grade protective coatings --- functional but not differentiated. To compete in the advanced specialty coatings market, Kastor would need to develop proprietary formulations through independent R&D. Estimated timeline: 3--5 years, with no guarantee of equivalent performance. Estimated cost: \$30M--\$50M.
>
> • North American market access. Kastor\'s North American presence in specialty polymer coatings is minimal. Its existing sales are primarily in Europe and Asia. Building a North American customer base from scratch --- particularly in the relationship-driven aerospace and industrial segments --- would require significant investment in sales infrastructure, technical support, and customer qualification. Estimated timeline: 2--3 years. Estimated cost: \$10M--\$15M.

**What Kastor Retains Without the JV:**

> • Its €38M in cash, available for redeployment to other projects (including the Ludwigshafen expansion and the South Korea JV)
>
> • Its manufacturing process documentation and polymer precursor supply chain
>
> • Its European and Asian distribution networks

**Asymmetry Assessment:** Kastor needs Meridian\'s IP more than Meridian needs Kastor\'s cash. Meridian\'s coating formulations are the JV\'s raison d\'être --- without them, there is no MKPoly. Kastor\'s cash contribution, while substantial, is fungible: Meridian could, in principle, raise equivalent capital through debt financing or alternative partnerships. Kastor cannot replicate Meridian\'s IP through any means other than multi-year R&D at uncertain cost and outcome.

This asymmetry is Meridian\'s most important source of negotiating leverage on IP license-back. Cordero should be prepared to invoke it, carefully, if Kastor pushes hard on sublicensing: \"We understand your interest in broad licensing, but let\'s be candid about what each of us brings to this partnership. Our formulations are the reason we\'re sitting at this table. We\'re not going to put them at risk.\"

**[APPENDIX B --- SCHEDULE 12.05 RESTRICTED COMPETITORS (PROPOSED DRAFT)]{.underline}**

**[Framework and Methodology]{.underline}**

Schedule 12.05 of the JVA will identify the Restricted Competitors to which neither party may sublicense co-developed IP (or, in the Second Concession structure, to which the revenue-based negative covenant applies as a supplementary restriction). The schedule serves as the primary enforcement mechanism for Meridian\'s IP sublicensing protections and must be drafted with care.

The following framework should be used to populate the schedule:

**Inclusion Criteria:** An entity should be listed on Schedule 12.05 if it meets any of the following criteria:

> \(a\) The entity derives 10% or more of its consolidated annual revenue from the manufacture, sale, or distribution of specialty polymer coatings in North America;
>
> \(b\) The entity has been identified by Meridian\'s competitive intelligence function as a direct competitor in any of MKPoly\'s target product categories (advanced protective coatings, performance coatings for aerospace applications, industrial specialty coatings);
>
> \(c\) The entity has publicly announced or is reasonably believed to be developing coating formulations competitive with Meridian\'s CR-7 or CR-9 systems; or
>
> \(d\) The entity is a controlled affiliate (defined as an entity in which the listed competitor owns, directly or indirectly, 50% or more of the outstanding voting equity) of any entity meeting criteria (a), (b), or (c).

**Notes on Schedule Maintenance:**

> \(a\) The schedule should be updated by Meridian\'s competitive intelligence team with current market information no later than August 8, 2025, three days prior to the principals meeting.
>
> \(b\) The JVA should include a mechanism for updating Schedule 12.05 during the JV term to capture new market entrants or corporate restructurings. Meridian\'s recommended mechanism: either party may propose additions to the schedule by written notice; additions become effective 60 days after notice unless the other party objects, in which case the dispute is resolved by the Board (subject to the casting-vote provision on non-Reserved Matters).
>
> \(c\) The definition of \"controlled affiliate\" should be drafted broadly to capture indirect ownership, including ownership through intermediary entities, trusts, and investment funds. The 50% threshold is recommended as the standard for control, but Meridian should consider a lower threshold (e.g., 25%) if there is concern that a Restricted Competitor could structure a sublicensing arrangement through a minority-owned affiliate.
>
> \(d\) The schedule should include a catch-all provision: \"any successor, assign, or entity that acquires all or substantially all of the assets or business of any entity listed in items 1 through 15 above.\" This ensures that corporate restructurings (e.g., a Restricted Competitor\'s sale of its coatings division to a new entity) do not create gaps in the schedule.

**Revenue-Based Negative Covenant (Supplementary Protection):**

In addition to the named Restricted Competitors on Schedule 12.05, the Second Concession IP license-back structure includes a revenue-based negative covenant: Kastor covenants not to sublicense co-developed IP to any entity that derives more than 25% of its consolidated annual revenue from the manufacture, sale, or distribution of specialty polymer coatings in North America. This covenant operates as a dynamic anti-circumvention mechanism that captures entities not named on Schedule 12.05, including:

> • New market entrants formed after the schedule was finalized
>
> • Existing companies that expand into specialty polymer coatings after the schedule was finalized
>
> • Companies that restructure their operations to avoid triggering the named-entity list
>
> • Foreign companies with significant North American coatings operations that were not identified during initial schedule preparation

The 25% revenue threshold is calibrated to capture entities with material North American coatings exposure while avoiding overinclusiveness. At 25%, the covenant captures any company for which North American specialty polymer coatings represent a significant line of business, while excluding diversified chemicals companies for which coatings are a minor, incidental product category. Meridian should resist any Kastor attempt to raise this threshold above 33%, as higher thresholds would exclude mid-sized competitors with substantial but not dominant coatings operations.

**Priority Ranking**

Let me be explicit about how I'm ranking these five items, because I want the=
 balance of concessions in your draft to reflect it:

1. **IP license-back (Section 12.05)** =E2=80=94 Highest priority. Non-negoti=
able red line on sublicensing.
2. **Management committee / board composition (Section 5.01)** =E2=80=94 Impo=
rtant, but room for compromise.
3. **Exit valuation / EBITDA multiple (Sections 10.03=E2=80=9310.04)** =E2=80=
=94 Flexible.
4. **Non-compete (Section 11.01)** =E2=80=94 Acceptable at a compromise durat=
ion.
5. **Capital call cure period (Section 4.03)** =E2=80=94 Acceptable at a comp=
romise period.

Hold the line on 1 and 2. Be more generous on 3, 4, and 5. That's the trade a=
rchitecture.

---

**1. IP License-Back =E2=80=94 Highest Priority**

This is where I will not bend. We are contributing 23 IP items independently =
appraised by Frazier Whitmore at $28.6M =E2=80=94 including proprietary coati=
ng formulations that will be embedded in JV products. Kastor is contributing =
9 items, primarily manufacturing process documentation. I am not going to acc=
ept any structure that allows Kastor to sublicense co-developed IP to our Nor=
th American competitors. Period.

Our opening position has been exclusive, royalty-free, perpetual license to M=
eridian only, with no sublicensing by Kastor. Kastor wants non-exclusive, roy=
alty-free, perpetual licenses to both parties with territorial sublicensing r=
ights =E2=80=94 broad co-developed IP license-back is their top priority.

Here's where I can move: Meridian can accept non-exclusive licenses to both p=
arties for their own internal use. But sublicensing must be restricted. At mi=
nimum, Kastor must be prohibited from sublicensing to any entity listed on Sc=
hedule 12.05 (Restricted Competitors) or any entity primarily competing in th=
e North American specialty polymer coatings market. That's the floor.

Both sides have flagged the improvements gap. Our contributed IP (the $28.6M =
portfolio) is going to be subject to JV-funded improvements over time =E2=80=
=94 I need you to address how co-developed IP and improvements on contributed=
 IP are handled in the draft provisions. The distinction matters. Contributed=
 IP reversion on exit is agreed by both sides, so no issue there.

Craft language that protects our core interest =E2=80=94 no competitive subli=
censing in North America =E2=80=94 while giving Kastor a meaningful co-develo=
ped IP license-back outside that constraint. They have a legitimate interest =
in using what the JV creates. I just need to make sure it doesn't end up in t=
he hands of our competitors through them.

Relevant references: Section 12.05, Schedule 12.01 (Contributed IP), Schedule=
 12.02 (Co-Developed IP), Schedule 12.05 (Restricted Competitors).

---

**2. Management Committee / Board Composition =E2=80=94 Important**

Our position is 3-2 board composition reflecting our 55/45 equity split. Kast=
or wants 3-3 =E2=80=94 equal representation despite unequal equity. For conte=
xt: our total contribution is $42.9M ($28.6M IP + $14.3M cash); Kastor's is $=
43.8M ($41.8M cash at the =E2=82=AC38M conversion plus $2.0M supply agreement=
 value). Total JV capitalization of $86.7M. The contributions are near parity=
 in dollar terms, but we hold majority equity, and the board should reflect t=
hat.

The 3-2 structure is important to me, but I recognize Kastor may need somethi=
ng in return. I can accept a Kastor veto on enumerated Reserved Matters requi=
ring supermajority or unanimous consent =E2=80=94 if the list is kept narrow.=
 My read from the last round is that Kastor has flagged board composition num=
bers as an area of greater flexibility on their side. They may concede 3-2 if=
 they get meaningful supermajority protections on Reserved Matters. That's a =
trade I can live with, as long as you keep the Reserved Matters list tight. D=
on't let them load it with operational decisions =E2=80=94 budget approvals, =
hiring below C-suite, ordinary-course contracts. Reserved Matters should be l=
imited to the genuinely existential items: capital structure changes, related=
-party transactions, IP disposition, dissolution, that category.

On deadlock: our position is CEO casting vote, Kastor wants mediation then fo=
rced buyout. I want Meridian's majority-appointed member to retain some form =
of tie-breaking mechanism on operational (non-Reserved) matters. On Reserved =
Matters, I understand deadlock resolution needs to be more balanced =E2=80=94=
 explore a compromise structure, but don't give away operational tie-breaking=
 entirely.

Reference Section 5.01.

---

**3. Exit Valuation / EBITDA Multiple =E2=80=94 Flexible**

Our opening position has been 6.5=C3=97 trailing 12-month EBITDA. Kastor has =
been pushing for a higher multiple and independent valuation. The Frazier Whi=
tmore comparable transactions analysis shows a range of 6.2=C3=97=E2=80=938.1=
=C3=97 with a median of 7.1=C3=97.

I'm comfortable moving to 7.0=C3=97. That's a meaningful concession =E2=80=94=
 we're essentially meeting Kastor near the market median, supported by indepe=
ndent data. The Frazier Whitmore analysis gives us solid ground to stand on.

On appraiser selection: our position has been the JV's own auditor (Frazier W=
hitmore). Kastor proposes a jointly-selected independent Big Four firm, with =
each side picking one appraiser, those two selecting a third, and baseball ar=
bitration. I understand Kastor's independence concern =E2=80=94 the auditor a=
rguably shouldn't also serve as valuation appraiser. I'm open to discussing a=
ppraiser selection mechanics, but see if you can propose something simpler th=
an the three-appraiser baseball arbitration. A single jointly-selected indepe=
ndent firm, perhaps with a fallback selection mechanism if we can't agree, wo=
uld be preferable.

On trigger events: we've proposed change of control and material breach. Kast=
or wants those plus deadlock and involuntary transfer. I'll leave this to you=
r legal judgment, but I'm open to including deadlock and involuntary transfer=
 as triggers if the valuation mechanics are fair and the 7.0=C3=97 multiple (=
or a reasonable independent process) governs.

Reference Sections 10.03=E2=80=9310.04.

---

**4. Non-Compete =E2=80=94 Acceptable at Compromise**

Our opening: 2-year non-compete covering all specialty polymer coatings in No=
rth America. Kastor's opening: 1 year, limited to specific JV product lines, =
limited to territories where the JV had active sales at exit.

I can accept 18 months. That sits comfortably within the Delaware and Texas e=
nforceability safe harbor of 1=E2=80=932 years in commercial contexts, and si=
nce MKPoly is a Delaware LLC with operations HQ in Houston, we're on solid gr=
ound. (I know California generally prohibits non-competes but has LLC/partner=
ship exceptions =E2=80=94 flag this if any JV operations touch California.)

The real fight here is scope. Kastor's "specific JV product lines" is too nar=
row =E2=80=94 it invites them to develop adjacent products the day after exit=
. My "all specialty polymer coatings" may be too broad to enforce. I want you=
 to draft something in between: JV product lines plus reasonably adjacent pro=
ducts, defined with enough specificity to be enforceable but broad enough to =
provide real protection. On geography, I want meaningful North American cover=
age =E2=80=94 I have some flexibility on exactly how that's defined, but it n=
eeds to be substantive, not just the three metro areas where the JV happens t=
o have salespeople.

Reference Section 11.01.

---

**5. Capital Calls / Cure Period and Dilution =E2=80=94 Acceptable at Comprom=
ise**

Our opening: 30-day cure period, uncapped immediate pro-rata dilution, no loa=
n alternative. Kastor's opening: 90-day cure period, dilution capped at 10% p=
er annum, loan alternative at market rate.

I can extend the cure period to approximately 75 days if dilution mechanics r=
emain meaningful. The whole point of dilution is to incentivize timely fundin=
g. Kastor's 10% annual dilution cap effectively removes the economic conseque=
nces of not funding =E2=80=94 that's unacceptable. I'm open to some reasonabl=
e structure, perhaps a moderated dilution formula, but it needs to create rea=

On the loan alternative: my instinct is that it undermines the dilution mecha=
nism. But I want your assessment of whether a limited loan provision =E2=80=
=94 a short-term bridge, maybe 30 days, that converts to dilution if not repa=
id =E2=80=94 could work as a compromise element without gutting the incentive=

mplications or loopholes, leave it out.

Reference Section 4.03.

---

**Next Steps**

I need the draft compromise provisions delivered to Escalante by August 6. Ea=
ch of the five contested terms =E2=80=94 management committee (5.01), capital=
 calls (4.03), non-compete (11.01), exit valuation (10.03=E2=80=9310.04), and=
 IP license-back (12.05) =E2=80=94 should be presented as a standalone provis=
ion with negotiation rationale, per Escalante's request. Also reference Sched=
ules 2.04 (Business Purpose), 12.01 (Contributed IP), 12.02 (Co-Developed IP)=
, and 12.05 (Restricted Competitors) as applicable.

One important note: this email is attorney-client privileged. The rationale d=
ocument going to Escalante should present a balanced middle ground =E2=80=94 =
it should not reveal our internal flexibility or priority ranking. The compro=
mise language should reflect the ranking I've outlined here (hold firm on IP =
and management, be more generous on valuation, non-compete, and capital calls=

I'm available tomorrow afternoon or Monday morning if you want to talk throug=

Elaine

02, 12.05). The balance of concessions across all five terms should reflect b=
